1.explanation:
Cách dùng : – Danh từ đứng trước は là chủ đề hoặc chủ ngữ trong câu.

– です được sử dụng cuối câu khẳng định, biểu lộ sự lịch sự đối với người nghe.

– Đứng trước です là một danh từ hoặc tính từ.

Chú ý: は khi là trợ từ được đọc là wa, không phải ha

Cách dùng: – ではありません là dạng phủ định của です. – Trong hội thoại người ta có thể dùng じゃありません thay cho ではありません

Chú ý : では đọc là dewa

examples.jp: ラオさんは エンジニアでは（じゃ）ありません。
examples.vi: Anh Rao không phải là kỹ sư.

1.meaning: 1) Câu hỏi nghi vấn (câu hỏi Có Không)
1.explanation:
1) Câu hỏi nghi vấn (câu hỏi Có Không)

2) Câu hỏi có từ để hỏi

Cách dùng: – Để tạo một câu hỏi chỉ cần thêm か vào cuối câu. – Câu trả lời cho loại câu hỏi này luôn phải có はい (vâng, đúng) hoặc いいえ (không, không phải ). Nếu giản lược đi bị xem là thất lễ.

Chú ý: Khi nói thì lên giọng ở trợ từ か

1.meaning: Cách dùng: – Trong tiếng Nhật sử dụng chữ さん đứng ngay sau tên của người nghe hoặc người thứ 3 để bày tỏ sự kính trọng đối với người đó. – Chữ さん không bao giờ
1.explanation:
Cách dùng: – Trong tiếng Nhật sử dụng chữ さん đứng ngay sau tên của người nghe hoặc người thứ 3 để bày tỏ sự kính trọng đối với người đó. – Chữ さん không bao giờ sử dụng sau tên của chính mình.

Chú ý: Khi trao đổi trực tiếp thì người Nhật ít sử dụng あなた khi đã biết tên của người nghe, mà sẽ dùng tên để gọi. Ngoài ra, ở Nhật khi gọi một người nào đó thì gọi nguyên cả tên và họ hoặc chỉ cần gọi họ là đủ. Chỉ gọi tên trong những trường hợp bạn bè quen thân hay người thân trong gia đình.

examples.jp: わたしは たなかです。
examples.vi: Tôi là Tanaka.

examples.jp: あのかたは きむらさんです。
examples.vi: Vị kia là Kimura.

Đứng sau số để chỉ tuổi. Viết bằng kanji là 歳.

Chú ý: Khi hỏi tuổi trang trọng dùng おいくつですか.

examples.vi: Tôi 20 tuổi.

2.explanation:
Cách dùng: – この、その、あの là các từ chỉ thị bổ nghĩa cho danh từ. Về tương quan khoảng cách thì giống với これ、それ、あれ nhưng khác về cách sử dụng vì luôn phải có danh từ đi liền đằng sau.

– “このN” dùng để chỉ vật hoặc người ở gần người nói, xa người nghe.

– “そのN” dùng để chỉ vật hay người ở gần người nghe, xa người nói.

– “あのN” dùng để chỉ vật hay người ở xa cả hai người.

Chú ý: なん là từ để hỏi dùng cho vật, だれ là từ để hỏi dùng cho người. これ/それ/あれ は N です

Chú ý: Khi một vật ở gần cả hai người thì cả hai người đều có thể dùng これ hay この

examples.jp: これは 本ですか。 これは ほ んですか。Đây là quyển sách à? …いいえ、それはノートです。 …
examples.vi: Không, đó là quyển vở.

examples.jp: あれは じどうしゃです。

2.pattern: そうです/そうではありません
2.explanation: Cách dùng: – そう được sử dụng để trả lời câu hỏi nghi vấn mà tận cùng là danh từ. – Trong câu khẳng định dùng: はい、そうです。 Trong câu phủ định dùng: いいえ、そうでは(じゃ)ありません。 Chú ý: Trong trường hợp câu nghi vấn mà tận cùng là động từ hay tính từ thì không sử dụng そうです hay そうではありません để trả lời.

examples.jp: 1) これは えんぴつですか。
examples.vi: Đây là cái bút chì phải không? …はい、えんぴつです。 …Vâng, đó là cái bút chì. Hoặc …はい、そうです。 …Vâng, đúng vậy. 2) それは テレホンカードですか。 Đó là cái th ẻ điện thoại phải không? …いいえ、テレホンカードではありません。 …Không, không phải cái thẻ điện thoại. Hoặc …いいえ、そうではありません。 …Không, không phải thế.

2.pattern: N1 ですか、N2 ですか
2.explanation: Chú ý: Khi trả lời câu hỏi này không dùng はいhay いいえ

Cách dùng: Ở bài trước, N1 là một tổ chức mà N2 thuộc vào đó. Ở bài này trợ từ の có ý nghĩa chỉ sự sở hữu. N2 thuộc sở hữu của N1

Chú ý: – N2 đôi khi được lược bỏ khi đã được nhắc đến trước đó hay đã rõ nghĩa. – Khi N2 là một từ chỉ người thì không được bỏ.

examples.jp: これは わたしの ほんです。
examples.vi: Đây là quyển sách của tôi.

examples.vi: Kia là cái cặp của ai? … わたしのです。 … Là của tôi.

examples.jp: 2) そのつくえは ラオさんのですか。
examples.vi: Cái bàn đó là của Rao phải không? … いいえ、ラオさんのではありません。 … Không, không phải của Rao.

examples.jp: 3) ミラーさんは
examples.vi: IMCのしゃいんですか。 Mira là nhân viên công ty IMC phải không? … はい、IMCのしゃいんです。 (Không dùng : IMCのです) … Vâng, (anh ấy) là nhân viên công ty IMC.

2.explanation: Cách dùng: Sử dụng khi người nói nhận được thông tin mới và thể hiện rằng đã hiểu về nó.

examples.jp: A: このかさは あなたのですか。
examples.vi: Cái ô này là của bạn à? B: いいえ、タンさんのです。 …Không, của anh Tân. A: そうですか。 À, ra vậy.

3.pattern: ここ／そこ／あそこ／こちら／そちら／あちら
3.meaning: The demonstratives ここ, そこ and あそこ refer to places.
3.explanation:
The demonstratives ここ, そこ and あそこ refer to places.

- ここ indicates the place where the speaker is.
- そこ indicates the place where the listener is.
- あそこ indicates a place distinct from both the speaker and the listener.

こちら, そちら and あちら refer to direction and can also be used as politer-sounding alternatives for ここ, そこ and あそこ.

3.meaning: Using this sentence pattern, you can state where a place, thing or person is.
3.explanation: Using this sentence pattern, you can state where a place, thing or person is.

examples.jp: お<ruby>手<rp>（</rp><rt>て</rt><rp>）</rp>洗<rp>（</rp><rt>あら</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>いは あそこです。
examples.vi: The toilet is over there.

examples.jp: <ruby>電<rp>（</rp><rt>でん</rt><rp>）</rp>話<rp>（</rp><rt>わ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は ２<ruby>階<rp>（</rp><rt>かい</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>です。
examples.vi: The telephone is on the second floor.

examples.jp: <ruby>山<rp>（</rp><rt>やま</rt><rp>）</rp>田<rp>（</rp><rt>だ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>さんは <ruby>事<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp>務<rp>（</rp><rt>む</rt><rp>）</rp>所<rp>（</rp><rt>しょ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>です。
examples.vi: Mr.Yamada is in his office.

3.meaning: どこ is used for asking "Where?" and どちら for "Which direction?" どちら can also be used for asking "Where?", in which case it
3.explanation:
どこ is used for asking "Where?" and どちら for "Which direction?" どちら can also be used for asking "Where?", in which case it is politer than どこ.

どこ and どちら are also used for asking the name of the country, company, school or other place or organization to which someone belongs. なん cannot be used in this case. どちら is politer than どこ.

examples.jp: お<ruby>手<rp>（</rp><rt>て</rt><rp>）</rp>洗<rp>（</rp><rt>あら</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>いは どこですか。
examples.vi: Where's the toilet? …… あそこです。 …… It's over there.

examples.jp: エレベーターは どちらですか。
examples.vi: Where's the elevator? …… あちらです。 …… It's in that direction. (It's over there.)

examples.jp: <ruby>学校<rp>（</rp><rt>がっこう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は どこですか。
examples.vi: What school do you go to? <ruby>会社<rp>（</rp><rt>かいしゃ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は どちらですか。 What company do you work for?

3.meaning: When N<sub>1</sub> is the name of a country/company and N<sub>2</sub> is the name of a product, it means that N<sub>2</s
3.explanation: When N<sub>1</sub> is the name of a country/company and N<sub>2</sub> is the name of a product, it means that N<sub>2</sub> is made in/by that country/company. The interrogative どこ is used when asking where or by what company something is made.

examples.jp: これは どこの コンピューターですか。
Where is this computer made? / What company is the computer made by?
examples.vi: …… <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>に</rt><rp>）</rp>本<rp>（</rp><rt>ほん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は コンピューターです。 …… It's made in Japan. …… パワー<ruby>電<rp>（</rp><rt>でん</rt><rp>）</rp>気<rp>（</rp><rt>き</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>の コンピューターです。 …… It's made by Power Electric Company.

3.pattern: The こ／そ／あ／ど system of demonstratives
3.meaning: |                                   | こ series | そ series | あ series | ど series
3.explanation:
| | こ series | そ series | あ series | ど series | |-----------------------------------|----------|----------|----------|---------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------| | Thing | これ | それ | あれ | どれ ([L.16](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_16_Grammar.md)) | | Thing / Person | この N | その N | あの N | どの N ([L.16](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_16_Grammar.md)) | | Place | ここ | そこ | あそこ | どこ | | Direction /
Place (polite) | こちら | そちら | あちら | どちら |

3.meaning: The prefix お is added to things related to the listener or a third party in order to show the speaker’s respect toward t
3.explanation: The prefix お is added to things related to the listener or a third party in order to show the speaker’s respect toward that person.

examples.jp: ［お］<ruby>国<rp>（</rp><rt>くに</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は どちらですか。
examples.vi: What country are you from?

4.pattern: <ruby>今<rp>（</rp><rt>いま</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> ー<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ー<ruby>分<rp>（</rp><rt>ふん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>です
4.meaning: To express time, the counter suffixes <ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>(o'clock) and <ruby>分<rp>（</rp><rt>ふん<
4.explanation:
To express time, the counter suffixes <ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>(o'clock) and <ruby>分<rp>（</rp><rt>ふん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>(minutes) are placed after the numbers.

- 分 is read ふん after 2, 5, 7, or 9.
- 分 is read ぷん after 1, 3, 4, 6, 8 and 10.
- 1, 6, 8 and 10 are read いっ, ろっ, はっ, and じゅっ（じっ） before ぷん.
- To ask time, なん is placed in front of じ or ぷん.

examples.jp: <ruby>今<rp>（</rp><rt>いま</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> <ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby><ruby><ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby><ruby>ですか。
examples.vi: What time is it now? …… ７<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>１０<ruby>分<rp>（</rp><rt>ぷん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>です。 …… It's 7:10.

4.pattern: V ます／V ません／V ました／V ませんでした
4.meaning: V ます works as a predicate. Using ます shows politeness toward the listener.
4.explanation:
V ます works as a predicate. Using ます shows politeness toward the listener.

V ます is used when a sentence expresses something habitual or a truth. It is also used when a sentence expressed a behavior or event that will occur in the future. The table below shows its negative and past-tense forms.

|                | Non-past (present or future) | Past             |
|----------------|------------------------------|------------------|
| Affirmative| V ます                    | V ました      |
| Negative   | V ません                  | V ませんでした |

Verb question sentences are formed by putting か at the end of sentence, without changing the word order. When using an interrogative, this is placed in the part of the sentence that the speaker wants to ask about. When answering such questions, the verb in the question is repeated. そうです and ちがいます (see [Lesson 2](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_02_Grammar.md)) cannot be used when replying to a verb sentence question.

examples.jp: わたしは <ruby>毎日<rp>（</rp><rt>まいにち</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> <ruby>勉強<rp>（</rp><rt>べんきょう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>します。
examples.vi: I study every day.

examples.jp: <ruby>毎朝<rp>（</rp><rt>まいあさ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> ６<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>に <ruby>起<rp>（</rp><rt>お</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます。
examples.vi: I get up at six every morning.

examples.jp: <ruby>明日<rp>（</rp><rt>あした</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> ６<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>に <ruby>起<rp>（</rp><rt>お</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます。
examples.vi: I am going to get up at six tomorrow morning.

examples.jp: <ruby>今朝<rp>（</rp><rt>けさ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> ６<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>に <ruby>起<rp>（</rp><rt>お</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きました。
examples.vi: I got up at six this morning.

examples.jp: <ruby>昨日<rp>（</rp><rt>きのう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> <ruby>勉強<rp>（</rp><rt>べんきょう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>しましたか。
examples.vi: Did you study yesterday? …… はい、<ruby>勉強<rp>（</rp><rt>べんきょう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>しました。 …… Yes, I did [study]. …… いいえ、<ruby>勉強<rp>（</rp><rt>べんきょう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>しませんでした。 …… No, I didn't [study].

examples.jp: <ruby>毎朝<rp>（</rp><rt>まいあさ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> <ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なん</rt><rp>）</rp>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>に <ruby>起<rp>（</rp><rt>お</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きますか。
examples.vi: What time do you get up in the mornings? …… ６<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>に <ruby>起<rp>（</rp><rt>お</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます。 …… [I get up at] six o'clock.

4.meaning: The particle に is appended to a noun indicating time to indicate the time of occurence of an action.
4.explanation: The particle に is appended to a noun indicating time to indicate the time of occurence of an action.

examples.jp: ６<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby><ruby>半<rp>（</rp><rt>はん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>に <ruby>起<rp>（</rp><rt>お</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます。
examples.vi: I get up at six-thirty.

examples.jp: ７<ruby>月<rp>（</rp><rt>がつ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby><ruby>２日<rp>（</rp><rt>ふつか</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>に <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>に</rt><rp>）</rp>本<rp>（</rp><rt>ほん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>来<rp>（</rp><rt>き</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ました。
examples.vi: I come to Japan on the second of July. (See [Lesson 5](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_05_Grammar.md).)

examples.jp: きのう <ruby>勉強<rp>（</rp><rt>べんきょう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>しました。
examples.vi: I studied yesterday.

examples.jp: <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>にち</rt><rp>）</rp>曜<rp>（</rp><rt>よう</rt><rp>）</rp>日<rp>（</rp><rt>び</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>に <ruby>奈良<rp>（</rp><rt>なら</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます。
examples.vi: I am going to Nara on Sunday. (See [Lesson 5](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_05_Grammar.md).)

4.meaning: から indicates a starting time or place, and まで indicates the finishing time or place.
4.explanation:
から indicates a starting time or place, and まで indicates the finishing time or place.

から and まで are not always used together.

To indicate the starting or finishing time/date of a noun introduced as a topic, 〜から、〜まで or 〜から〜まで can be used with「です」attached.

examples.jp: ９<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>から ５<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>まで <ruby>勉強<rp>（</rp><rt>べんきょう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>します。
examples.vi: I study from 9 to 5.

examples.jp: <ruby>大<rp>（</rp><rt>おお</rt><rp>）</rp>阪<rp>（</rp><rt>さか</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>から <ruby>東<rp>（</rp><rt>とう</rt><rp>）</rp>京<rp>（</rp><rt>きょう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>まで ３<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp>間<rp>（</rp><rt>かん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> かかります。
examples.vi: It takes three hours from Osaka to Tokyo. (See [Lesson 11](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_11_Grammar.md).)

examples.jp: ９<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>から <ruby>働<rp>（</rp><rt>はたら</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます。
examples.vi: I start work at nine.

examples.jp: <ruby>銀<rp>（</rp><rt>ぎん</rt><rp>）</rp>行<rp>（</rp><rt>こう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は ９<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>から ３<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>までです。
examples.vi: The bank is open from 9 to 3.

examples.jp: <ruby>昼<rp>（</rp><rt>ひる</rt><rp>）</rp>休<rp>（</rp><rt>やす</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>みは １２<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>からです。
examples.vi: The lunch break starts at 12.

4.meaning: The particle と connects two nouns in coordinated relation.
4.explanation: The particle と connects two nouns in coordinated relation.

examples.jp: <ruby>銀<rp>（</rp><rt>ぎん</rt><rp>）</rp>行<rp>（</rp><rt>こう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> <ruby>休<rp>（</rp><rt>やす</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>みは <ruby>土<rp>（</rp><rt>ど</rt><rp>）</rp>曜<rp>（</rp><rt>よう</rt><rp>）</rp>日<rp>（</rp><rt>び</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>と <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>にち</rt><rp>）</rp>曜<rp>（</rp><rt>よう</rt><rp>）</rp>日<rp>（</rp><rt>び</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>です。
examples.vi: The bank is closed on Saturdays and Sundays.

4.meaning: The particle ね is attached to the end of a sentence and is used to elicit agreement from the listener, check that the li
4.explanation: The particle ね is attached to the end of a sentence and is used to elicit agreement from the listener, check that the listener has understood, or emphasize something to the listener.

examples.jp: <ruby>毎日<rp>（</rp><rt>まいにち</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> １０<ruby>時<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>まで <ruby>勉強<rp>（</rp><rt>べんきょう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>します。
examples.vi: I study till ten every day. …… <ruby>大変<rp>（</rp><rt>だいへん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ですね。 …… That's tough, isn't it?

examples.jp: <ruby>山<rp>（</rp><rt>やま</rt><rp>）</rp>田<rp>（</rp><rt>だ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>さんの <ruby>電<rp>（</rp><rt>でん</rt><rp>）</rp>話<rp>（</rp><rt>わ</rt><rp>）</rp>番<rp>（</rp><rt>ばん</rt><rp>）</rp>号<rp>（</rp><rt>ごう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は ８７１の ６８１３です。
examples.vi: Mr.Yamada's telephone number is 871-6813. …… ８７１の ６８１３ですね。 …… 871-6813, right?

5.pattern: N(place) へ <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます／<ruby>来<rp>（</rp><rt>き</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ます／<ruby>帰<rp>（</rp><rt>かえ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ります
5.meaning: When a verb indicates movement to a certain place, the particle へ is put after the place noun to show the direction of t
5.explanation: When a verb indicates movement to a certain place, the particle へ is put after the place noun to show the direction of the movement.

examples.jp: <ruby>京<rp>（</rp><rt>きょう</rt><rp>）</rp>都<rp>（</rp><rt>と</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます。
examples.vi: I am going to Kyoto.

examples.jp: <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>に</rt><rp>）</rp>本<rp>（</rp><rt>ほん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>来<rp>（</rp><rt>き</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きました。
examples.vi: I came to to Japan.

examples.jp: うちへ <ruby>帰<rp>（</rp><rt>かえ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ります。
examples.vi: I am going home.

5.pattern: どこ[へ]も <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きません／<ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きせんでした
5.meaning: When you want to deny everything covered by an interrogative, you can attach the particle も to the interrogative and put
5.explanation: When you want to deny everything covered by an interrogative, you can attach the particle も to the interrogative and put the verb in its negative form.

examples.jp: どこ[へ]も <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きません。
examples.vi: I am not going anywhere.

examples.jp: <ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なに</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>も <ruby>食<rp>（</rp><rt>た</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>べません。
examples.vi: I am not going to eat anything. (See [Lesson 6](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_06_Grammar.md).)

examples.jp: だれも <ruby>来<rp>（</rp><rt>き</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ませんでした。
examples.vi: Nobody came.

5.pattern: N(vehicle) で <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます／<ruby>来<rp>（</rp><rt>き</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ます／<ruby>帰<rp>（</rp><rt>かえ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ります
5.meaning: The particle で indicates a means or a method. The speaker attaches it after a noun representing a vehicle and uses it to
5.explanation:
The particle で indicates a means or a method. The speaker attaches it after a noun representing a vehicle and uses it together with a movement verb to indicate his or her means of transport.

When talking about walking somewhere, the speaker uses the expression あるいた. In this case, the particle で is not used.

examples.jp: <ruby>電<rp>（</rp><rt>でん</rt><rp>）</rp>車<rp>（</rp><rt>しゃ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>で <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます。
examples.vi: I am going by train.

examples.jp: タクシーで <ruby>来<rp>（</rp><rt>き</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ました。
examples.vi: I came by taxi.

examples.jp: <ruby>駅<rp>（</rp><rt>えき</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>から <ruby>歩<rp>（</rp><rt>ある</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>いて <ruby>帰<rp>（</rp><rt>かえ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>りました。
examples.vi: I walked home from the station.

5.meaning: When talking about doing something with a person or an animal, the person or animal is marked with the particle と.
5.explanation:
When talking about doing something with a person or an animal, the person or animal is marked with the particle と.

If doing something by oneself, the expression ひとりで is used. In this case, the particle と is not used.

examples.jp: <ruby>家<rp>（</rp><rt>か</rt><rp>）</rp>族<rp>（</rp><rt>ぞく</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>と <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>に</rt><rp>）</rp>本<rp>（</rp><rt>ほん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>来<rp>（</rp><rt>き</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ました。
examples.vi: I came to Japan with my family.

examples.jp: <ruby>一人<rp>（</rp><rt>ひとり</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>で <ruby>東<rp>（</rp><rt>とう</rt><rp>）</rp>京<rp>（</rp><rt>きょう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます。
examples.vi: I am going to Tokyo on my own.

5.meaning: To ask about time, interrogatives using なん, such as なんじ, なんようび, なんがつ, なんにち are used. The interrogative いつ (when) is also
5.explanation: To ask about time, interrogatives using なん, such as なんじ, なんようび, なんがつ, なんにち are used. The interrogative いつ (when) is also used to ask when something will happen or happened. いつ does not take the particle に.

examples.jp: いつ <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>に</rt><rp>）</rp>本<rp>（</rp><rt>ほん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>来<rp>（</rp><rt>き</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ましたか。
examples.vi: When did you come to Japan? …… ３<ruby>月<rp>（</rp><rt>がつ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>２５<ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>にち</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>に <ruby>来<rp>（</rp><rt>き</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ました。 …… [I came] on 25th March.

examples.jp: いつ <ruby>広<rp>（</rp><rt>ひろ</rt><rp>）</rp>島<rp>（</rp><rt>しま</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きますか。
examples.vi: When are you going to Hiroshima? …… <ruby>来週<rp>（</rp><rt>らいしゅう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます。 …… [I am going] next week.

5.meaning: The particle よ is placed at the end of a sentence. It is used to emphasise information which the listener does not know,
5.explanation: The particle よ is placed at the end of a sentence. It is used to emphasise information which the listener does not know, or to show the speaker is giving his/her judgement or views assertively.

examples.jp: この <ruby>電<rp>（</rp><rt>でん</rt><rp>）</rp>車<rp>（</rp><rt>しゃ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は <ruby>甲<rp>（</rp><rt>こう</rt><rp>）</rp>子<rp>（</rp><rt>し</rt><rp>）</rp>園<rp>（</rp><rt>えん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きますか。
examples.vi: Does this train go to Koshien? …… いいえ、<ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きません。<ruby>次<rp>（</rp><rt>つぎ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>の「<ruby>普<rp>（</rp><rt>ふ</rt><rp>）</rp>通<rp>（</rp><rt>つう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>」ですよ。 …… No, it does not. You need the next local train.

examples.jp: <ruby>北海道<rp>（</rp><rt>ほっかいどう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>に <ruby>馬<rp>（</rp><rt>うま</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>が たくさん いますよ。
examples.vi: There are a lot of horses in Hokkaido, you know. (See [Lesson 18](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_18_Grammar.md).)

examples.jp: マリアさん、この アイスクリーム、おいしいですよ。
examples.vi: Maria, this ice cream is very nice, you know. (See [Lesson 19](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_19_Grammar.md).)

5.meaning: The expression そうですね is used to express sympathy or agreement with what the speaker has said. It is similar to the expre
5.explanation: The expression そうですね is used to express sympathy or agreement with what the speaker has said. It is similar to the expression そうですか (see [Lesson 2](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_02_Grammar.md)), but while そうですか is used by a speaker to acknowledge the receipt of some new information, そうですね is used to show sympathy or agreement with something the speaker already knew.

examples.jp: <ruby>明日<rp>（</rp><rt>あした</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>にち</rt><rp>）</rp>曜<rp>（</rp><rt>よう</rt><rp>）</rp>日<rp>（</rp><rt>び</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ですね。
examples.vi: It is Sunday tomorrow, isn't it? …… あ、そうですね。 …… Oh, yes, so it is.

6.meaning: The particle を is used to indicate the direct object of a transitive verb.
6.explanation: The particle を is used to indicate the direct object of a transitive verb.

examples.jp: ジュースを <ruby>飲<rp>（</rp><rt>の</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>みです。
examples.vi: I drink juice.

6.meaning: A fairly wide range of nouns are used as the objects of the verb します, which means that the action denoted by the noun is
6.explanation:
A fairly wide range of nouns are used as the objects of the verb します, which means that the action denoted by the noun is performed. Some examples are shown below.

- Play sports or games

- Hold gatherings or events

- Do something

examples.jp: サッカーを します
examples.vi: play soccer

examples.jp: トランプを　します
examples.vi: play cards

examples.jp: パーティーを します
examples.vi: give a party

examples.jp: <ruby>会<rp>（</rp><rt>かい</rt><rp>）</rp>議<rp>（</rp><rt>ぎ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を します
examples.vi: hold a meeting

examples.jp: <ruby>宿<rp>（</rp><rt>しゅく</rt><rp>）</rp>題<rp>（</rp><rt>だい</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を します
examples.vi: do homework

examples.jp: <ruby>仕<rp>（</rp><rt>し</rt><rp>）</rp>事<rp>（</rp><rt>ごと</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を します
examples.vi: work

examples.jp: <ruby>電<rp>（</rp><rt>でん</rt><rp>）</rp>話<rp>（</rp><rt>わ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を します
examples.vi: call, phone

6.meaning: This is a question to ask about someone's actions.
6.explanation: This is a question to ask about someone's actions.

examples.jp: <ruby>月<rp>（</rp><rt>げつ</rt><rp>）</rp>曜<rp>（</rp><rt>よう</rt><rp>）</rp>日<rp>（</rp><rt>び</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> <ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なに</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を しますか。
examples.vi: What are you doing on Monday? …… <ruby>京<rp>（</rp><rt>きょう</rt><rp>）</rp>都<rp>（</rp><rt>と</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます。 …… I am going to Kyoto.

examples.jp: <ruby>昨日<rp>（</rp><rt>きのう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> <ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なに</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を しましたか。
examples.vi: What did you do yesterday? …… サッカーを しました。 …… I played soccer.

6.meaning: Both なん and なに mean 'what'.
6.explanation:
Both なん and なに mean 'what'.

- なん is used in the following cases:

- When it precedes a word whose first is in the た, だ and な -row.

- なんで is used for asking 'Why?' as well as 'How?' なにで can be used when the speaker wants to make it clear the he or she is asking "How?"

- When using it with a counter suffix.

- なに is used in all other cases apart from above part.

examples.jp: これは <ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby><u>で</u>すか。
<ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby><u>の</u> <ruby>本<rp>（</rp><rt>ほん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ですか。
<ruby>寝<rp>（</rp><rt>ね</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>る まえに、<ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby><u>と</u> <ruby>言<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>いますか。
<ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby><u>で</u> <ruby>東<rp>（</rp><rt>とう</rt><rp>）</rp>京<rp>（</rp><rt>きょう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きますか。
<ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なに</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>で <ruby>東<rp>（</rp><rt>とう</rt><rp>）</rp>京<rp>（</rp><rt>きょう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きますか。
テレーザちゃんは <ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なん</rt><rp>）</rp>歳<rp>（</rp><rt>さい</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ですか。
<ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なに</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を <ruby>買<rp>（</rp><rt>か</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>いますか。

6.meaning: When add after a noun denoting a place, the particle で indicates the occurrence of an action in that place.
6.explanation: When add after a noun denoting a place, the particle で indicates the occurrence of an action in that place.

examples.jp: <ruby>駅<rp>（</rp><rt>えき</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>で <ruby>新<rp>（</rp><rt>しん</rt><rp>）</rp>聞<rp>（</rp><rt>ぶん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を <ruby>買<rp>（</rp><rt>か</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>います。
examples.vi: I am going to buy a paper at the station.

6.meaning: This expression is used when the speaker wants to invite someone to do something.
6.explanation: This expression is used when the speaker wants to invite someone to do something.

examples.jp: いっしょに <ruby>京<rp>（</rp><rt>きょう</rt><rp>）</rp>都<rp>（</rp><rt>と</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きませんか。
examples.vi: Would you like to come to Kyoto with us? …… ええ、いいですね。 …… Yes, that's a nice idea.

6.meaning: This expression is used when a speaker is positively inviting the listener to do something with the speaker. It is also
6.explanation: This expression is used when a speaker is positively inviting the listener to do something with the speaker. It is also used when responding positively to an invitation.

examples.jp: ちょっと <ruby>休<rp>（</rp><rt>やす</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>みましょう。
examples.vi: Let's take a break.

examples.jp: いっしょに <ruby>昼<rp>（</rp><rt>ひる</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ごはんを <ruby>食<rp>（</rp><rt>た</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>べませんか。
examples.vi: Shall we have lunch together? …… ええ、<ruby>食<rp>（</rp><rt>た</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>べましょう。 …… Yes, let's do it.

6.meaning: か indicates that the listener has received and accept some new information. It is used in the same way as the か in そうですか
6.explanation: か indicates that the listener has received and accept some new information. It is used in the same way as the か in そうですか (see [Lesson 2](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_02_Grammar.md)).

examples.jp: <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>にち</rt><rp>）</rp>曜<rp>（</rp><rt>よう</rt><rp>）</rp>日<rp>（</rp><rt>び</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> <ruby>京<rp>（</rp><rt>きょう</rt><rp>）</rp>都<rp>（</rp><rt>と</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>へ <ruby>行<rp>（</rp><rt>い</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きました。
examples.vi: I went to Kyoto on Sunday. …… <ruby>京<rp>（</rp><rt>きょう</rt><rp>）</rp>都<rp>（</rp><rt>と</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ですか。いいですね。 …… Kyoto, eh? Great!

7.meaning: The particle で indicates a method or means used for an action.
7.explanation: The particle で indicates a method or means used for an action.

examples.jp: <ruby>箸<rp>（</rp><rt>はし</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>で <ruby>食<rp>（</rp><rt>た</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>べます。
examples.vi: I eat with chopsticks.

examples.jp: <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>に</rt><rp>）</rp>本<rp>（</rp><rt>ほん</rt><rp>）</rp>語<rp>（</rp><rt>ご</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>で レポートを <ruby>書<rp>（</rp><rt>か</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>きます。
examples.vi: I am going to write the report in Japanese.

7.pattern: 'Word/Sentence' は 〜<ruby>語<rp>（</rp><rt>ご</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>で <ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ですか
7.meaning: This question is used to ask how to say a word or sentence in another language.
7.explanation: This question is used to ask how to say a word or sentence in another language.

examples.jp: 「ありがとう」は <ruby>英<rp>（</rp><rt>えい</rt><rp>）</rp>語<rp>（</rp><rt>ご</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>で <ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ですか。
examples.vi: What's 'Arigato' in English? …… 「Thank you」です。 …… It's 'thank you'.

examples.jp: 「Thank you」は <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>に</rt><rp>）</rp>本<rp>（</rp><rt>ほん</rt><rp>）</rp>語<rp>（</rp><rt>ご</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>で <ruby>何<rp>（</rp><rt>なん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ですか。
examples.vi: What's 'Thank you' in Japanese? …… 「ありがとう」です。 …… It's 'ありがとう'.

7.pattern: N<sub>1</sub>(person) に N<sub>2</sub> を あげます, etc
7.meaning: Verbs like あげます, かします and おしえます indicate imparting things or information, so they must be used with a noun saying to who
7.explanation: Verbs like あげます, かします and おしえます indicate imparting things or information, so they must be used with a noun saying to whom those things or information are imparted. The particle に is used to denote the recipient.

examples.jp: ［わたしは］<ruby>木<rp>（</rp><rt>き</rt><rp>）</rp>村<rp>（</rp><rt>むら</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>さんに <ruby>花<rp>（</rp><rt>はな</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を あげました。
examples.vi: I gave Ms.Kimura some flowers.

examples.jp: ［わたしは］イーさんに <ruby>本<rp>（</rp><rt>ほん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を <ruby>貸<rp>（</rp><rt>か</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>しました。
examples.vi: I lent Ms.Lee a book.

examples.jp: ［わたしは］<ruby>山<rp>（</rp><rt>やま</rt><rp>）</rp>田<rp>（</rp><rt>だ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>さんに <ruby>英<rp>（</rp><rt>えい</rt><rp>）</rp>語<rp>（</rp><rt>ご</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を <ruby>教<rp>（</rp><rt>おし</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>えます。
examples.vi: I teach Mr.Yamada English.

7.pattern: N<sub>1</sub>(person) に N<sub>2</sub> を もらいます, etc
7.meaning: Verbs like もらいます, かります and ならいます indicate receiving things or information, so they are used with a noun indicating the p
7.explanation: Verbs like もらいます, かります and ならいます indicate receiving things or information, so they are used with a noun indicating the person from whom those things or information are received. The particle に is used to denote that person.

examples.jp: ［わたしは］<ruby>山<rp>（</rp><rt>やま</rt><rp>）</rp>田<rp>（</rp><rt>だ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>さんに <ruby>花<rp>（</rp><rt>はな</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を もらいました。
examples.vi: I received some flowers from Mr.Yamada.

examples.jp: ［わたしは］カリナさんに ＣＤを <ruby>借<rp>（</rp><rt>か</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>りました。
examples.vi: I borrowed a CD from Karina.

examples.jp: ［わたしは］ワンさんに <ruby>中<rp>（</rp><rt>ちゅう</rt><rp>）</rp>国<rp>（</rp><rt>ごく</rt><rp>）</rp>語<rp>（</rp><rt>ご</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を <ruby>習<rp>（</rp><rt>なら</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>います。
examples.vi: I'm learning Chinese from Mr.Wang.

examples.jp: ［わたしは］<ruby>山<rp>（</rp><rt>やま</rt><rp>）</rp>田<rp>（</rp><rt>だ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>さんから <ruby>花<rp>（</rp><rt>はな</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を もらいました。
examples.vi: I received some flowers from Mr.Yamada.

examples.jp: <ruby>銀<rp>（</rp><rt>ぎん</rt><rp>）</rp>行<rp>（</rp><rt>こう</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>から お<ruby>金<rp>（</rp><rt>かね</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を <ruby>借<rp>（</rp><rt>か</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>りました。
examples.vi: I borrowed some money from the bank.

7.meaning: もう means 'already' and is used with V ました. In this case, V ました means that the action has been completed.
7.explanation:
もう means 'already' and is used with V ました. In this case, V ました means that the action has been completed.

The answer to the question もう V ましたか as to whether an action has been completed or not is:

- はい、もう V ました if in the affirmative (i.e. the action has been completed).
- いいえ、V て いません (see [Lesson 31](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_31_Grammar.md)) or いいえ、まだです if in the negative (i.e. the action has not been completed).
- いいえ、V ませんでした is not used in that case, since this means that something was not done in the past, rather than that something has not been completed in the present.

examples.jp: もう <ruby>荷<rp>（</rp><rt>に</rt><rp>）</rp>物<rp>（</rp><rt>もつ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>を <ruby>送<rp>（</rp><rt>おく</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>りましたか。
examples.vi: Have you sent the baggage yet? …… はい、［もう］<ruby>送<rp>（</rp><rt>おく</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>りました。 …… Yes, I have [sent it]. …… いいえ、まだ <ruby>送<rp>（</rp><rt>おく</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>って いません。 …… No, I haven't sent it yet. (See [Lesson 31](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_31_Grammar.md).) …… いいえ、まだです。 …… No, not yet.

7.meaning: Particles are often omitted in informal speech when the relationships between the parts of speech before and after them
7.explanation: Particles are often omitted in informal speech when the relationships between the parts of speech before and after them are obvious.

examples.jp: この スプーン［は］、すてきですね。
examples.vi: This is a lovely spoon, isn't it?

examples.jp: コーヒー［を］、もう <ruby>一杯<rp>（</rp><rt>いっぱい</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby> いかがですか。
examples.vi: Would you like another cup of coffee? (See [Lesson 8](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_08_Grammar.md).)

8.meaning: Adjectives are used as predicates, and in sentences like Nは adjです they indicates the state of a noun or are used to modi
8.explanation: Adjectives are used as predicates, and in sentences like Nは adjです they indicates the state of a noun or are used to modify a noun. They are divided into one of two groups, い-adjectives and な-adjectives, depending on how they inflect.

8.pattern:
N は な-adj［~な~］です
N は い-adj（〜い）です
8.meaning: Adjective sentences that are non-past and affirmative end in です, which shows politeness toward the listener. Both types
8.explanation:
Adjective sentences that are non-past and affirmative end in です, which shows politeness toward the listener. Both types of adjective are attached to the front of です, but な-adjectives drop their な, while い-adjectives retain their（〜い）.

The non-past negative of a な-adj is formed by dropping the な and attaching じゃ（では）ありません to it.

examples.jp: ワット<ruby>先<rp>（</rp><rt>せん</rt><rp>）</rp>生<rp>（</rp><rt>せい</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は <ruby>親<rp>（</rp><rt>しん</rt><rp>）</rp>切<rp>（</rp><rt>せつ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>です。
examples.vi: Mr.Watt is kind.

examples.jp: <ruby>富<rp>（</rp><rt>ふ</rt><rp>）</rp>士<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp>山<rp>（</rp><rt>さん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は <ruby>高<rp>（</rp><rt>たか</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>いです。
examples.vi: Mt.Fuji is high.

examples.jp: あそこは <ruby>静<rp>（</rp><rt>しず</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>かじゃ（では）ありません。
examples.vi: It is not quiet there.

8.meaning: The non-past negative of a い-adj is formed by dropping the final い and attaching くないです to it.
8.explanation: The non-past negative of a い-adj is formed by dropping the final い and attaching くないです to it.

examples.jp: この <ruby>本<rp>（</rp><rt>ほん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は <ruby>面<rp>（</rp><rt>おも</rt><rp>）</rp>白<rp>（</rp><rt>しろ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>くないです。
examples.vi: This book is not interesting.

8.explanation:
|                          |  な-adjectives             | い-adjectives |
|--------------------------|---------------------------|---------------|
| Non-past affirmative | しんせつです                | たかいです      |
| Non-past negative    | しんせつじゃ（では）ありません | たかくないです   |

8.meaning: Questions using adjective sentences are formed in the same way as those using noun sentences (see [Lesson 1](https://git
8.explanation: Questions using adjective sentences are formed in the same way as those using noun sentences (see [Lesson 1](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_01_Grammar.md)) and verb sentences (see [Lesson 4](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_04_Grammar.md)). To answer such a question, the adjective is repeated. Expressions such as そうです or ちがいます cannot be used.

examples.jp: ペキンは <ruby>寒<rp>（</rp><rt>さむ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>いですか。
examples.vi: Is it cold in Beijing? …… はい、<ruby>寒<rp>（</rp><rt>さむ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>いです。 …… Yes, it is [cold].

examples.jp: <ruby>奈<rp>（</rp><rt>な</rt><rp>）</rp>良<rp>（</rp><rt>ら</rt><rp>）</rp>公<rp>（</rp><rt>こう</rt><rp>）</rp>園<rp>（</rp><rt>えん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は にぎやかですか。
examples.vi: Is Nara Park busy? …… いいえ、にぎやかじゃ ありません。 …… No, it isn't [busy].

8.meaning: When an adjective is used to modify a noun, it is placed in front of the noun. な-adjectives keep their な in this case.
8.explanation: When an adjective is used to modify a noun, it is placed in front of the noun. な-adjectives keep their な in this case.

examples.jp: ワット<ruby>先<rp>（</rp><rt>せん</rt><rp>）</rp>生<rp>（</rp><rt>せい</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は <ruby>親<rp>（</rp><rt>しん</rt><rp>）</rp>切<rp>（</rp><rt>せつ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>な <ruby>先<rp>（</rp><rt>せん</rt><rp>）</rp>生<rp>（</rp><rt>せい</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>です。
examples.vi: Mr.Watt is a considerate teacher.

examples.jp: <ruby>富<rp>（</rp><rt>ふ</rt><rp>）</rp>士<rp>（</rp><rt>じ</rt><rp>）</rp>山<rp>（</rp><rt>さん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は <ruby>高<rp>（</rp><rt>たか</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>い <ruby>山<rp>（</rp><rt>やま</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>です。
examples.vi: Mt.Fuji is a high mountain.

8.meaning: が connects two statements in an antithetical relationship. When these are adjective clauses with the same subject, if th
8.explanation: が connects two statements in an antithetical relationship. When these are adjective clauses with the same subject, if the initial clause remarks on the subject positively, the subsequent clause will remark on it negatively, and vice versa.

examples.jp: <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>に</rt><rp>）</rp>本<rp>（</rp><rt>ほん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>の <ruby>食<rp>（</rp><rt>た</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>べ<ruby>物<rp>（</rp><rt>もの</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は おいしですが、<ruby>高<rp>（</rp><rt>たか</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>いです。
examples.vi: Japanese food is delicious, but it is expensive.

8.meaning: とても and あまり are both adverbs of degree, and both come before the adjectives they modify. とても is used in affirmative sent
8.explanation: とても and あまり are both adverbs of degree, and both come before the adjectives they modify. とても is used in affirmative sentences, and means 'very'. あまり, used with a negative, means 'not very'.

examples.jp: ペキンは とても <ruby>寒<rp>（</rp><rt>さむ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>いです。
examples.vi: Beijing is very cold.

examples.jp: これは とても <ruby>有<rp>（</rp><rt>ゆう</rt><rp>）</rp>名<rp>（</rp><rt>めい</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>な <ruby>映<rp>（</rp><rt>えい</rt><rp>）</rp>画<rp>（</rp><rt>が</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>です。
examples.vi: This is a very famous movie.

examples.jp: シャンヘイは あまり <ruby>寒<rp>（</rp><rt>さむ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>くないです。
examples.vi: Shanghai is not very cold.

examples.jp: <ruby>桜<rp>（</rp><rt>さくら</rt><rp>）</rp>大<rp>（</rp><rt>だい</rt><rp>）</rp>学<rp>（</rp><rt>がく</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は あまり <ruby>有<rp>（</rp><rt>ゆう</rt><rp>）</rp>名<rp>（</rp><rt>めい</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>な <ruby>大<rp>（</rp><rt>だい</rt><rp>）</rp>学<rp>（</rp><rt>がく</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>じゃ ありません。
examples.vi: Sakura University is not a very well-known university.

8.pattern: N は どうですか
8.meaning: The question N は どうですか is used to inquire about the listener's impression, option or feelings about a thing, place, pers
8.explanation: The question N は どうですか is used to inquire about the listener's impression, option or feelings about a thing, place, person, etc. that he or she has experienced, visited or met.

examples.jp: <ruby>日<rp>（</rp><rt>に</rt><rp>）</rp>本<rp>（</rp><rt>ほん</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>の <ruby>生<rp>（</rp><rt>せい</rt><rp>）</rp>活<rp>（</rp><rt>かつ</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は どうですか。
examples.vi: How do you find life in Japan? …… <ruby>楽<rp>（</rp><rt>たの</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>しいです。 …… It's fun.

8.pattern: N<sub>1</sub> は どんな N<sub>2</sub> ですか
8.meaning: どんな modifies a noun and is an interrogative used for inquring about the state or nature of a person, thing, etc.
8.explanation: どんな modifies a noun and is an interrogative used for inquring about the state or nature of a person, thing, etc.

examples.jp: <ruby>奈<rp>（</rp><rt>な</rt><rp>）</rp>良<rp>（</rp><rt>ら</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は どんな <ruby>町<rp>（</rp><rt>まち</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>ですか。
examples.vi: What sort of town is Nara? …… <ruby>古<rp>（</rp><rt>ふる</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>い <ruby>町<rp>（</rp><rt>まち</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>です。 …… It's an old town.

8.meaning: The use of the expression そうですね to express agreement or sympathy was explained in [Lesson 5](https://github.com/flying-y
8.explanation: The use of the expression そうですね to express agreement or sympathy was explained in [Lesson 5](https://github.com/flying-yogurt/JP-Memos/blob/master/grammar_notes/Lesson_05_Grammar.md). The そうですね that appears in the Conversation of this Lesson shows that the speaker is thinking, as in the below sentence.

examples.jp: お<ruby>仕<rp>（</rp><rt>し</rt><rp>）</rp>事<rp>（</rp><rt>ごと</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>は どうですか。
examples.vi: How's your work going? …… そうですね。<ruby>忙<rp>（</rp><rt>いそが</rt><rp>）</rp></ruby>しいですが、おもしろいです。 …… Well, it's busy, but it's interesting.

9.explanation:
「Ｎが好きです／嫌いです」
食べ物、スポーツ、音楽、映画などについて自分の嗜好を伝えるのに「好き／嫌い」の「形容動詞」を用いる。「好き／嫌い」の対象は助詞「が」で示す。
「～好きじゃありません」=「嫌いです」ですが、「嫌い」は直接的で聞き手に悪印象を与えるので、注意が必要です。
「嫌いです」は強い印象があり、誤解を招くことも多いので、軽く触れる程度とし、「あまり好きじゃありません」を練習する。否定形が「嫌いじゃありません」。
「どんな～か」に対する答えは形容詞を用いた答え方だけでなく具体的な名詞でも答えられる。
「どんなスポーツが好きですか」に対する答えは２つの答え方があります。
**具体的に名詞で答える**（例：テニス、サッカーなど）
**好きなスポーツの特徴を述べる答え方**（例：外でするスポーツ、ボールを使うスポーツ）

examples.jp: わたしは韓国料理が好きです。
クラシックコンサートが好きです。
母はカラオケが好きです。
ご主人は音楽が好きですか。

9.explanation: 「Ｎが上手です／下手です」 「上手／下手」は形容動詞であり、対象は助詞「が」で示す。 「私は歌が上手です」は一般的には言わない。「私は歌が得意です。」は OK。 「下手です」も意味が強すぎて使用頻度は少ない。実際は下手な場合でも「上手じゃありません」を使用することが多い。 「〇〇が上手ですか。」は親しい人にのみ使う。 日本人は自分のことに「～が上手だ」とは言わない。おこがましい。 「上手・下手」は技量、技術の優劣に使う。「勉強」「数学」「音楽」などには使えない。「勉強」などの場合には、その分野に自信があるという意味で「得意」が自然ですが、「得意」は未習の語彙なので、「好き・嫌い」を使うように指導しましょう。 相手に直接「～が上手ですか」と聞かないほうがいい。

examples.jp: ラビさんは歌が上手です。
高田さんは料理が上手です。
ミカエルさんはダンスが上手です。
エミさんは日本語が上手です。

9.explanation:
「Ｎがわかります」
目的語を取る動詞は原則そして目的語に助詞「を」をつけて示すが、「わかります」はその対象を助詞「が」で示す。
「よく／だいたい／少し／あまり／全然」などの程度を表す副詞は修飾する動詞や形容詞の前に置く。

examples.jp: トムさんは漢字がわかりますか。
…はい、わかります。
…いいえ、わかりません。
● わかります・わかりました

9.explanation:
「Ｎあります」
目的語を取る動詞は原則として助詞「を」をつけて示すが、「わかります」と同様に「あります」もその対象を助詞「が」で示す。ここでは「～があります」で物の所有の意味と時間、用事、約束などがある場合の用法を扱う。

examples.jp: お金と車があります。
サッカーのチケットがあります。
わたしは赤い車があります。
チョウさんはお金があります。

9.explanation:
「～から、～」
原因＋から、結果。
「どうして〜か」
疑問詞「どうして」で理由を尋ねる。答えの文の文末には「から」をつける。

examples.jp: 飲み物がありません**から**、コンビニで買います。
お金がありません**から**、バスで行きます。
コンサートのチケットがあります**から**、一緒に行きませんか。
ひまです**から**、ダンスの雑誌を読みます。

10.pattern: N があります/ いま
10.meaning: N があります/ いま

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: sentence pattern is used to indicate the existence or presence of a thing(s) or person(s). The thing(s) or person(s) in such a sentence is treated as the subject and marked with the particle が.

examples.jp: コンピューターがあります。
examples.vi: There is a computer.

examples.jp: 桜があります。
examples.vi: There are cherry trees.

examples.jp: 公園があります。
examples.vi: There is a park.

examples.jp: 男の人がいます。
examples.vi: There is a man.

examples.jp: 犬がいます。
examples.vi: There is a dog.

10.pattern: N1 (place)に N2 が あります/ います
10.meaning: N1 (place)に N2 が あります/ います

examples.jp: わたしの部屋に机があります。
examples.vi: There is a desk in my room.

examples.jp: 事務所にミラーさんがいます。
examples.vi: Mr.Miller is in the office.

examples.jp: 地下に何がありますか。
examples.vi: What is there in the basement?

examples.jp: …レストランがあります。 …There
examples.vi: are restaurants.

examples.jp: 受付にだれがあります。
examples.vi: Who is at the reception desk?

examples.jp: …木村さんがいます。 …
examples.vi: Ms.Kimura is there.

10.pattern: N1 は N2 (place) に あります/ います
10.explanation:
Where is Tokyo Disneyland?

…It’s in Chiba Prefecture.

Where is Tokyo Disneyland?

…It’s in Chiba Prefecture.

examples.jp: 東京ディズニーランドは千葉県にあります。
examples.vi: Tokyo Disneyland is in Chiba Prefecture.

examples.jp: みらーさんは事務所にいます。
examples.vi: Mr.Miller is in the office.

examples.jp: 東京ディズニーランドはどこにありますか。
… 千葉県に あります。
ミラーさんはどこにいますか。
examples.vi: Where is Mr.Miller?

examples.jp: …事務所にいます。 …He’s
examples.vi: in the office.

examples.jp: 東京ディズニーランドはどこにありますか。
… 千葉県です。
うえ, した, まえ, うしろ, みぎ, ひだり, なか, そと, となり, ちかく
examples.vi: and あいだ are nouns denoting position.

examples.jp: 机の上に写真があります。
examples.vi: There is a picture on the desk.

examples.jp: 郵便局は銀行の隣 (となり) にあります。
examples.vi: The post office is next to the bank.

examples.jp: 駅の近くで友達に会いました。
examples.vi: I met a friend near the station.

examples.jp: Nouns
examples.vi: are concerned in coordinate relation by the particle や. While と enumerates all the items, や shows a few representative items. Sometimes など is put after the last noun to explicitly express that there are also some other things of the kind.

examples.jp: 箱 (はこ) の中に手紙 (てがみ)や写真 (しゃしん)があります。
examples.vi: There are letters, pictures and so on in the box.

examples.jp: 箱 (はこ) の中に手紙 (てがみ)や写真 (しゃしん)などがあります。
examples.vi: There are letters, pictures and so on in the box.

10.meaning: Excuse me, bt where is Yunyu-ya Store?
10.explanation:
Excuse me, bt where is Yunyu-ya Store?

…Yunya-yu Store? It’s in that building.

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: particle か has the function to confirm. The speaker picks up a word or words he/she wants to confirm and confirms it (them) using this pattern.

examples.jp: すみません。ユニューヤ・ストアはどこですか。
…ユニューヤ・ストアですか。あのビルの なかです。

10.pattern: チリソースはありませんか
10.meaning: チリソースはありませんか

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: expression チリソースはありませんか is found in the conversation of this lesson. By using the negative form ありませんか instead of ありますか, you can be indirect and polite, showing that you are prepared for a negative answer.

11.meaning: With this expression you can how often you do something.
11.explanation:
With this expression you can how often you do something.

I go to see movies twice a month.

examples.jp: 1ヵ月に2回映画を見ます。

11.pattern: Quantifier だけ / N だけ
11.meaning: There is only one foreign employee in Power Electric.
11.explanation:
There is only one foreign employee in Power Electric.

I only have Sundays off.

examples.jp: だけ
examples.vi: means “only”. It is added after quantifiers or nouns to express that there is no more or nothing ( no one ) else.

examples.jp: パワー電気に外国人の社員が1人だけいます
休みは日曜日だけです。

12.pattern: Past tense of noun sentences and な- adjective sentences.
12.meaning: Past tense of noun sentences and な- adjective sentences.

examples.jp: Affirmative:
examples.vi: N/Na adj + です － N/Na adj + でした。Negative: N/Na adj + じゃ（では）ありません。 – N/Na adj + じゃ（では）ありませんでした.

examples.jp: きのうは雨でした。
examples.vi: It was rainy yesterday

examples.jp: きのうの試験は簡単じゃありませんでした。Yesterday’s
examples.vi: exam was not easy.

12.pattern: Past tense of い adjective sentences Affirmative: あついです。 － あつかったです Negative: あつくないです － あつくなかったです
12.meaning: Past tense of い adjective sentences Affirmative: あついです。 － あつかったです Negative: あつくないです － あつくなかったです

examples.jp: きのうは暑かったです。
examples.vi: It was hot yesterday きのうのパーティーはあまり楽しくなかったです。 I didn’t enjoy yesterday’s party very much

12.pattern: N1 は N2 よりadjectiveです.
12.meaning: This sentence pattern describes the quality and/ or state of N1 in comparison with N2.
12.explanation:
This sentence pattern describes the quality and/ or state of N1 in comparison with N2.

This car is bigger than that car.

examples.jp: この車はあの車より大きいです

12.pattern: N1 とN2とどちらがAdjectiveですか
12.meaning: N1 とN2とどちらがAdjectiveですか
Which is more interesting, baseball or football?
12.explanation:
Which is more interesting, baseball or football?

Who is a better tennis player, Mr. Miller or Mr. Santos?

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: question asks the listener to choose between two items ( N1 and N2). The interrogative used is always どちらif the comparison is made between two items.

examples.jp: サッカーと野球とどちらがおもしろいですか。
サッカーのほうがおもしろいです。
ミラーさんとサントスさんとどちらがテニスが上手ですか

12.pattern: N1の中で何・どこ・だれ・いつ・がいちばんadjectiveですか
12.meaning: N1の中で何・どこ・だれ・いつ・がいちばんadjectiveですか

This question pattern is used to ask the listener to choose something that is the most “adjective”. The choice is made form the group or category denoted by N1. The interrogative used is decided by th
12.explanation:
This question pattern is used to ask the listener to choose something that is the most “adjective”. The choice is made form the group or category denoted by N1. The interrogative used is decided by the kind of category from which the choice is made.

Among Japanese dishes, what is the most delicious?

When is the coldest time of the year?

It’s coldest in February.

examples.jp: 日本料理の中で何がいちばんおいしいですか。
天ぷらがいちばんおいしいです。
１年でいつがいちばん寒いですか
２月がいちばん寒いです。
When
examples.vi: the subject of an adjective sentence is questioned, が is attached to the interrogative in the same way.

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: sentence pattern is used to express the speaker’s desire to possess or have an object. It can also be used to ask what the listener wants. The object is marked with the particle が. ほしい is an い- adjective.

examples.jp: わたしは友達が欲しいです。
examples.vi: I want a friend.

examples.jp: 今何が一番欲しいですか。What
examples.vi: do you want most now?

examples.jp: 車が欲しいです. ….I
examples.vi: want a car most.

examples.jp: 子どもが欲しいですか。
examples.vi: Do you want a child?

examples.jp: いいえ、欲しくないです。…No,
examples.vi: I don’t

13.pattern: V ます-form たいです.
13.meaning: V ます-form たいです.

examples.jp: 1)Verb ます-form
The
examples.vi: form of a verb when it is used with ます is called the ます form. In the word かいます かい is the ます form.
V ますform たいです

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: expresses the speaker’s desire to do something. It is also used to ask what the listener wants to do. In this expression, as is seen in the example below , the particle が can replace the particle を。The other particles cannot be replaced by がVますform-たい inflects as an い-adjective.

examples.jp: わたしは沖縄へ行きたいです。I
examples.vi: want to go to Okinawa

examples.jp: わたしはてんぷらを（が）食べたいです。I
examples.vi: want to eat tempura

examples.jp: 神戸で何を（が）買いたいですか。What
examples.vi: do you want to buy in Kobe.

examples.jp: 靴を（が）買いたいです。I
examples.vi: want to by a pair of shoes

examples.jp: お腹が痛いですから、何も食べたくないです。Because
examples.vi: I have a stomachache, I don’t want to eat anything.

13.pattern: N(place) へ Vます-form/ N に 行きます/来ます/帰ります。
13.meaning: I’ll go to the festical in Kyoto tomorrow.
13.explanation: I’ll go to the festical in Kyoto tomorrow.

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: purpose for 行きます/来ます/帰ります is expessed using this pattern. The purpose is marked with the particle に . A noun used before に is of the kind of denoting an action.

examples.jp: 神戸へインド料理を食べに行きます。I’m
examples.vi: going to Kobe to eat Indian food.

examples.jp: 神戸へ買い物に行きます。I’m
examples.vi: going to Kobe for shopping.

examples.jp: 日本へ美術の勉強に来ました。I
examples.vi: came to Japan in order to study art.

examples.jp: あした京都のお祭りに行きます。
The
examples.vi: particle にmarks the goal when used with verbs like はいります、のります、etc. The particle を marks the starting point or place when used with verbs like でます、おります, etc.

examples.jp: あの喫茶店に入りましょう。Let’s
examples.vi: go in that coffee shop.

examples.jp: 7時にうちを出ます。I
examples.vi: leave my house at 7 o’clock.

13.meaning: Did you go anywhere in the winter vacation?
13.explanation:
Did you go anywhere in the winter vacation?

I’m thirsty. I want to drink something.

examples.jp: どこか
examples.vi: means anywhere or somewhere. 何か means anything or something. The particles へ and を can me omitted.

examples.jp: 冬休みはどこか「へ」行きましたか。
。。。はい、行きました。
のどがかわしましたから、何か「を」飲みたいです。
ご
examples.vi: is a prefix added to some words to express respect.

examples.jp: ご注文は？
examples.vi: May I have your order?

14.meaning: Verbs in Japanese change their forms, i.e., they conjugate, and they are divided into three groups according to the type of conjugation. Depending on the following phrases, you can make sentences with
14.explanation: Verbs in Japanese change their forms, i.e., they conjugate, and they are divided into three groups according to the type of conjugation. Depending on the following phrases, you can make sentences with various meanings.

1) Group I verbs

2) Group II verbs

3)Group III verbs

examples.jp: In
examples.vi: the verbs of this group, the last sound of the ます – form is that of the い-line.

examples.jp: かきます
examples.vi: write のみます drink.

examples.jp: In
examples.vi: most of the verbs of this group , last sound of the ます-form is that of the え- line, but in some verbs the last sound of the ます-form is that of the い-line.

examples.jp: たべます
examples.vi: eat みせます show みます see

examples.jp: Verbs
examples.vi: of this group include します and “noun denoting an action + します” as well as きます。

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: verb form which ends with て or で is called the て- form. How to make the て-form of a verb depends on which group the verb belongs to as explained below.

examples.jp: 1)Group
examples.vi: I Depending on the last sound of the ます-form, the て- form of the verbs of this group is made as shown in the form table. Note that the て- form of the verb いきます、いって , is an exception.

examples.jp: 2)Group
examples.vi: II Attach て to the ます-form.

examples.jp: 3)Group
examples.vi: III Attach て to the ます-form.

14.pattern: Vて-form ください : Please do…
14.meaning: This sentence pattern is used to ask, instruct or encourage the listener to do something. Naturally if the listener is one’s superior, this expression cannot be used for giving instructions to him/her
14.explanation:
This sentence pattern is used to ask, instruct or encourage the listener to do something. Naturally if the listener is one’s superior, this expression cannot be used for giving instructions to him/her. The sentences shown below are examples of asking, instructing and encouraging, respectively.

Excuse me, could you tell me how to read this kanji, please.?

Please write you name and address here.

Please come to my place.

examples.jp: すみませんが、この漢字の読み方を教えてください。
ここに住所と名前を書いてください
ぜひ遊びに来てください。
When
examples.vi: it is used to ask the listener to do something, すみませんが is often added before V て-form ください. This expression is pliter than only saying Vて-form ください.

14.pattern: Vて-form います : Be V-ing
14.meaning: This sentence pattern indicates that a certain action or motion us in progress.
14.explanation:
This sentence pattern indicates that a certain action or motion us in progress.

Mr Miller is making a phone call now.

examples.jp: ミラーさんは今電話をかけています。
今雨が降っていますか?
examples.vi: Is it raining now

examples.jp: 。。。はい、降っています。 …Yes,
examples.vi: it is.

examples.jp: 。。。いいえ、降っていません。 …No,
examples.vi: it is not.

14.pattern: Vます-form ましょか : Shall I…?
14.meaning: This expression is used when the speaker is offering to do something for the listener.
14.explanation:
This expression is used when the speaker is offering to do something for the listener.

In the above example conversations, B demontratres how to politely ask or instruct someone to do something, to accept an offer with gratitude and to decline an offer poitlely.

examples.jp: A.あしたも来ましょうか
examples.vi: Shall I come tomorrow, too?

examples.jp: B.ええ１０時にきてください。 ….Yes,
examples.vi: please come at ten.

examples.jp: A.傘を貸しましょうか
examples.vi: Shall I lend you an umbrella?

examples.jp: B.すみません。お願いします。 …Yes,
examples.vi: please.

examples.jp: A.荷物を持ちましょうか
examples.vi: Shall I carry your parcel?

examples.jp: B.いいえ、けっこうです。 …No,
examples.vi: thank you.

14.meaning: Excuse me, but may I have your name?
14.explanation:
Excuse me, but may I have your name?

Please pass me the salt.

examples.jp: 失礼ですが、お名前は？
すみませんが塩を取ってください。
You
examples.vi: learned the conjunctive particle が in lesson 8. In expressions such as しつれいですが orすみませんが , which are used as introductory remarks when speaking to someone, が , losing its original meaning, is used to connect two sentences lightly.

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: describing a natural phenomenon, the subject is indicated by が.

examples.jp: 雨が降っていますか.

15.pattern: Vて-form も いいです: You may do…
15.meaning: Vて-form も いいです: You may do…

examples.jp: 写真を撮ってもいいですか。
examples.vi: You may take pictures.

examples.jp: たばこを吸ってもいいですか。
examples.vi: May I smoke?

examples.jp: このカタログをもらってもいいですか。
examples.vi: May I have this catalogue?

examples.jp: …ええ、いいですよ。どうぞ。 …
examples.vi: Yes. Here you are.

examples.jp: …すみません。ちょっと。 …Sorry.
examples.vi: I’m afraid not.

15.pattern: Vて-formは いけません: You must not do…
15.meaning: You must not smoke here. Because this is no-smoking area.
15.explanation: You must not smoke here. Because this is no-smoking area.

examples.jp: 先生、ここで遊んでもいいですか。
examples.vi: May we play here, Ma’am?

examples.jp: …いいえ、いけません。 …
examples.vi: No, you must not.

examples.jp: In
examples.vi: addition to the usage of Vて-form いますyou learned in Lesson 14, it is also used in describing a certain continuing state which resulted from a certain action in the past.

examples.jp: わたしは結婚しています。
examples.vi: I’m married.

examples.jp: わたしは田中さんを知っています。
examples.vi: I know Mr. Tanaka.

examples.jp: わたしは大阪に住んでいます。
examples.vi: I live in Osaka.

examples.jp: わたしはかめらをもっています。
examples.vi: I have a camera.

examples.jp: もっています
examples.vi: means “be holding” and “possess” as well.

examples.jp: Vて-form
examples.vi: is also used in describing a habitual action; that is, when the same action is repeatedly performed over a period of time.

examples.jp: Therefore,
examples.vi: one’s occupation can be expressed by this sentence pattern. So if the question おしごとはなんですか is asked, this sentence pattern can be used to answer it.

examples.jp: IMC はコンピューターを作っています。
examples.vi: IMC makes computer software.

examples.jp: スーパーでフィルムを打っています。
examples.vi: Supermarkets sell films.

examples.jp: ミラーさんは
examples.vi: IMCで働いています。 Mr. Miller works for IMC.

examples.jp: 妹は 大学で勉強しています。
examples.vi: My younger sister is studying at university.

15.meaning: Do you know the telephone number of the city hall? … Yes, I do.
15.explanation: Do you know the telephone number of the city hall? … Yes, I do.

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: negative form of しっています is しりません.

examples.jp: 市役所の 電話番後を知っていますか。
…はい、知っています。
…いいえ、知りません。

16.meaning: In the morning, I jog, take a shower and go to the office.
16.explanation:
In the morning, I jog, take a shower and go to the office.

I went to Kobe, saw a movie and drank tea.

examples.jp: To
examples.vi: connect verb sentences, the て-form is used. When two or more actions take place in succession, the actions are mentioned in the order of occurrence by using the て-form. The tense of sentence is determined by the tense form of the last verb in the sentence.

examples.jp: 朝ジョギングをしてシャワーを浴びて、会社へ行きます。
神戸へいって映画を見て、お茶を飲みました。

16.meaning: Mr Miller is young and lively.
16.explanation:
Mr Miller is young and lively.

Yesterday it was fine and hot.

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: joining an い-adjective sentence to another sentence, take away the い from the い-adjective and attach くて。

examples.jp: おおき-い => おおきくて
ちいさ-い => ちいさくて
い-い => よくて
ミラーさんは若くて元気です。
きのうは天気がよくて、暑かったです。

16.meaning: Ms Karina is an Indonesian and a student of Kyoto University.
16.explanation:
Ms Karina is an Indonesian and a student of Kyoto University.

Mr Miller is handsome and kind.

Nara is a quiet and beautiful city.

Ms Karina is a student and Maria is a housewife.

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: joining noun sentences or な-adjective sentences, です is changed to で.

examples.jp: カリナさんはインドネシア人で京都大学の留学生です。
ミラーさんはハンサムで親切です。
奈良は静かで、きれいな街です。
カリナさんは学生で、マリアさんは主婦です。
X : この部屋は狭くて、きれいです。
O :この部屋は狭いですが、きれいです。This
examples.vi: room is small but clean.

16.meaning: This sentence pattern indicates that upon completion of the action denoted by V1 the action of V2 os to be conducted. The tense of the sentence is determined bu the tense form of the last verb in the
16.explanation:
This sentence pattern indicates that upon completion of the action denoted by V1 the action of V2 os to be conducted. The tense of the sentence is determined bu the tense form of the last verb in the sentence.

I will work for my father’s company after going back to my country.

We ate at a restaurant after the concert was over.

examples.jp: 国へ帰ってから父の会社で働きます。
コンサートが終わってから、レストランで食事します。

16.explanation:
Food is tasty in Osaka.

Franken in Germany produces famous wine.

Maria has long hair.

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: sentence pattern is used to describe an attribute of a thing or a person. The topic of the sentence is denoted by は, N1 is the topic of the sentence. N2 is the subject of the adjective’s description.

examples.jp: 大阪は食べ物がおいしいです。
ドイツのフランケンはワインが有名です
マリアさんは髪が長いです。

16.meaning: How do you go to your university?
16.explanation:
How do you go to your university?

…I take a No.16 bus from Kyoto Station and get off at the front of the university.

examples.jp: どうやってis
examples.vi: used to ask the way or the method of doing something. To answer such a question, the pattern learned in 1. is used.

examples.jp: 大学までどうやって行きますか。
京都駅から１６番のバスに乗って、大学前で降ります。

16.explanation:
Which one is Mr. Santos?

…That tall man with black hair.

examples.jp: You
examples.vi: learned in lesson 2 that この、その and あの modify nouns. The interrogative word used in this system is どの。どの is used to ask the listener to define one among more than two which are concretely presented.

examples.jp: サントスさんはどの人ですか。
…あの背が高くて、髪が黒いです。
The
examples.vi: verb form used withない is called the ない-form; that is to say, かかof かかないis the ない-form of かきます(write). How to make the ない-form is given below.

examples.jp: In
examples.vi: the verbs of this group the last sound of the ます-form is always the sound in the い-line. So, replace it with the sound of the あ-line to make a ない-form. The exceptions to this rule are such verbs as かいます, あいます, etc. (わis the last sound of the ない-form in the verbs instead of あ).

examples.jp: かき – ます → かか – ない いそぎ – ます → いそが – ない
よみ – ます → よま – ない あそび – ます → あそば – ない
とり – ます → とら – ない まち – ます → また – ない
すい – ます → すわ – ない はなし – ます → はなさ – ない
The ない-form
examples.vi: of verbs of this group is just the same as the ます-form.

examples.jp: たべ – ます → たべ – ない
み – ます → み – ない
The ない-form
examples.vi: ofします is the same as the ます-form. きます becomes こ(ない).

examples.jp: べんきょうし – ます → べんきょうし – ない
し – ます → し – ない
き – ます → こ – ない

17.pattern: Vない-form ないでください
17.meaning: This expression is used to ask or instruct someone not to do something.
17.explanation:
This expression is used to ask or instruct someone not to do something.

I am fine, so please don’t worry about me.

Please don’t take pictures here.

examples.jp: わたしは元気ですから、心配しないでください。
ここで写真を撮らないでください。

17.pattern: Vない-formなければなりません
17.meaning: This expression means something has to be done regardless of the will of the actor. Note that this doesn’t have a negative meaning.
17.explanation:
This expression means something has to be done regardless of the will of the actor. Note that this doesn’t have a negative meaning.

I must take medicine.

examples.jp: 薬を飲まなければなりません。

17.pattern: Vない-form なくてもいいです
17.meaning: This sentence pattern indicates that the action described by the verb does not have to be done.
17.explanation:
This sentence pattern indicates that the action described by the verb does not have to be done.

You don’t have to come tomorrow.

examples.jp: あした来なくてもいいです。

17.explanation:
Please don’t put parcels here.

As for parcels, don’t put them here.

I have lunch in the company cafeteria.

As for lunch, I have it in the company cafeteria.

examples.jp: You
examples.vi: learned in Lesson 6 that the particleを is attached to the direct object of verbs. Here you learn that the object is made a topic by replacingを with は.

examples.jp: ここに荷物を置かないでください。
荷物はここに置かないでください。
会社の食堂で昼ごはんを食べます。
昼ごはんは会社の食堂で食べます。

17.meaning: The meeting will be over by five.
17.explanation:
The meeting will be over by five.

I must return the book by Saturday.

I work until five.

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: point in time indicated by までにis the time limit by which an action is to be done.

examples.jp: 会議は5時までに終わります。
土曜日までに本を返さなければなりません。
5時まで働きます。

18.meaning: This form is the basic form of a verb. Verbs are given in this form in the dictionary, hence the name. How to make the dictionary form is given below.
18.explanation: This form is the basic form of a verb. Verbs are given in this form in the dictionary, hence the name. How to make the dictionary form is given below.

Mr. Miller can speak Japanese.

It’s snowed a lot, so we can ski this year.

Mr. Miller can read Kanji.

You can pay by credit card.

examples.jp: できますis
examples.vi: the verb which expresses ability or possibility. A noun and V dictionary form ことbefore がindicates the content of ability or possibility.

examples.jp: Nouns
examples.vi: placed before がare mostly nouns which express actions such as driving a car, shopping, skiing, dancing, etc. Nouns such as にほんご, which is associated with the action はなす, or ピアノ, which is associated with the actionひく, can also be used here.

examples.jp: ミラーさんは日本語ができます。
雪がたくさん降りましたから、ことしはスキーができます。
When
examples.vi: a verb is used to describe ability or possibility, ことshould be attached to the dictionary form of the verb to make it a nominalized phrase and then ができますis put after that.

examples.jp: ミラーさんは漢字を読むことができます。
カードで払うことができます。

18.explanation:
My hobby is music.

My hobby is listening to the music.

examples.jp: As
examples.vi: shown in the sentences below, V dictionary form ことcan express the content of hobbies more concretely than the noun alone can do.

examples.jp: わたしの趣味は音楽です。
わたしの趣味は音楽を聞くことです。

18.meaning: This sentence pattern indicates that the action of V2 occurs before the action of V1 takes place. Even when the tense V2 is in the past tense or the future tense, V1 is always in the dictionary form.
18.explanation:
This sentence pattern indicates that the action of V2 occurs before the action of V1 takes place. Even when the tense V2 is in the past tense or the future tense, V1 is always in the dictionary form.

I studied Japanese before I came to Japan.

I read a book before I go to bed.

I wash my hands before eating.

3) Quantifier (period)

Mr. Tanaka left an hour ago.

examples.jp: 日本へ来るまえに、日本語を勉強しました。
寝るまえに、本を読みます。
When まえにcomes
examples.vi: after a noun, the particleの is put between the noun and まえに. Nouns before まえにare nouns which express actions or nouns which imply actions.

examples.jp: 食事のまえに、手を洗います。
Whenまえに
examples.vi: comes after a quantifier (period), the particleの is not necessary.

examples.jp: 田中さんは1時間まえに、出かけました。

18.meaning: In Japan we can rarely see horses.
18.explanation: In Japan we can rarely see horses.

examples.jp: Whenなかなか
examples.vi: is accompanied by a negative expression, it means “not easily” or “not as expected”.

examples.jp: 日本ではなかなか馬を見ることができません。

18.meaning: I want to go to Hokkaido very much.
18.explanation:
I want to go to Hokkaido very much.

Please come to my place.

examples.jp: ぜひis
examples.vi: used with expressions of hope and request such as ほしいです, Vます-form たいですand Vて-form くださいand emphasizes the meaning of the expressions.

examples.jp: ぜひ北海道へ行きたいです。
ぜひ遊びに来てください。
In
examples.vi: this lesson you learn the た-form. How to make the た-form is shown below.

examples.jp: The た-form
examples.vi: is made by changing てand でof the て-form into たand だrespectively.

examples.jp: のんで かいた
のんだ
examples.vi: Group II たべて たべた Group III きて

examples.jp: して きた
した

19.explanation:
have the experience of V-ing

I have ridden a horse.

Note that it is, therefore, different from a sentence which merely states the fact that one did something at a certain time in the past.

I rode a horse in Hokkaido last year.

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: sentence pattern is used to describe what one has experienced in the past. This is basically the same sentence as わたしはNがあります which you learned in Lesson 9. The content of one’s experience is expressed by the nominalized phrase Vた-form こと.

examples.jp: 馬に乗ったことがあります。
去年北海道で馬に乗りました。

19.pattern: Vた-formり, Vた-form りします
19.meaning: V … and V …, and so on
19.explanation:
V … and V …, and so on

On Sundays, I play tennis, see a movie and so on.

Last Sunday, I played tennis, saw a movie and so on.

Last Sunday I played tennis and then saw a movie.

In (**) it is clear that seeing a movie took place after playing tennis. In (*) there is no time relation between the two activities. These activities are mentioned as example activities among the activities done on Sunday to imply that one did other activities besides them. And it is not natural that actions usually done by everybody every day such as getting up in the morning, taking meals, going to bed at night, etc., are mentioned.

examples.jp: You
examples.vi: learned an expression for referring to a few things and persons among many (～や～ [ など ] ) in Lesson 10. The sentences learned here are used in referring to some actions among many other actions. The tense of this sentence pattern is shown at the end of the sentence.

examples.jp: 日曜日はテニスをしたり、映画を見たりします。
日曜日はテニスをしたり、映画を見たりしました。(*)
日曜日テニスをして、映画を見ました。(**)
なりますmeans “become”
examples.vi: and indicates changes in a state or condition.

examples.jp: 寒い → 寒くなります
examples.vi: get cold

examples.jp: 元気[な] → 元気になります
examples.vi: get well

examples.jp: 25歳 → 25歳になります
examples.vi: become 25 years old

examples.jp: そうですねis
examples.vi: used when you agree or sympathize with what your partner in conversation said. そうですかwith a falling intonation is a similar expression to this (see Lesson 2, 6). そうですかis, however, an expression of your conviction or exclamation after getting information which was unknown to you, whileそうですね is used to express your agreement or sympathy with your partner in conversation when he/she refers to something you agree with or already know.

examples.jp: 寒くなりましたね。
そうですね。

20.meaning: Japanese language has two styles of speech: polite style and plain style.
20.explanation:
Japanese language has two styles of speech: polite style and plain style.

I have never been to Germany.

examples.jp: I
examples.vi: will go to Tokyo tomorrow. あした東京へ行く。

examples.jp: I
examples.vi: will go to Tokyo tomorrow. 毎日忙しいです。

examples.jp: I
examples.vi: am busy every day. 毎日忙しい。

examples.jp: I
examples.vi: am busy every day. 相撲が好きです。

examples.jp: I
examples.vi: like sumo. 相撲が好きだ。

examples.jp: I
examples.vi: like sumo. 富士山に登りたいです。

examples.jp: I
examples.vi: want to climb Mt. Fuji. 富士山に登りたい。

examples.jp: I
examples.vi: want to climb Mt. Fuji. ドイツへ行ったことがありません。

examples.jp: I
examples.vi: have never been to Germany. ドイツへ行ったことがない。

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: predicates which are used in polite style sentences and accompanied by either ですor ますare called the polite form, while the predicates used in plain style sentences are called the plain form.

20.pattern: Proper use of the polite style or the plain style
20.meaning: 1) The polite style can be used at anytime in any place and to anybody. Therefore, the polite style is used most commonly in daily conversation between adults who are not close friends. It is used whe
20.explanation:
1) The polite style can be used at anytime in any place and to anybody. Therefore, the polite style is used most commonly in daily conversation between adults who are not close friends. It is used when talking to a person one has met for the first time, to one’s superiors, or even to persons in a similar age group to whom one is not very close. The polite style may be chosen when one talks to a person who is younger or lower in rank yet not so close. The plain style is used when talking to one’s close friends, colleagues and family members.

Note that you need to be careful about how much politeness is needed, basing this on the age of your conversation partner and your type of relationship. If the plain style is used inappropriately, you could sound rough and impolite, so when you cannot tell the situation it is safer to use the polite style.

2) The plain style is commonly used in written work. Newspapers, books, theses and diaries are all written in the plain style. Most letters are written in the polite style.

20.meaning: 3) In the plain style, certain particles are often omitted if the meaning of the sentence is evident from the context.
20.explanation:
3) In the plain style, certain particles are often omitted if the meaning of the sentence is evident from the context.

Will you take a meal?

Won’t you come to Kyoto tomorrow with me?

This apple is tasty, isn’t it?

Is there a pair of scissors there?

Is that curry and rice tasty?

Yes, it’s hot but tasty.

I have tickets for sumo. Won’t you come with me?

examples.vi: Questions in the plain style generally omit the particle か, which denotes a question, and end with a rising intonation.

examples.jp: コーヒーを飲む？
examples.vi: Do you want a coffee?

examples.jp: うん、飲む。
examples.vi: Yes, I do.

In noun and な-adjective questions, だ, which is the plain form of です, is omitted. In an answer in the affirmative, ending the sentence with だcould sound too rough. You can either omit だor add some sentence final particle to soften the tone of the sentence. Women seldom use だ.

examples.jp: 今晩暇？
examples.vi: Are you free tonight? (used by both men and women)

examples.jp: うん、暇／暇だ／暇だよ。
examples.vi: Yes, I am. (used by men)

examples.jp: うん、暇／暇よ。
examples.vi: Yes, I am. (used by women)

examples.jp: ううん、暇じゃない。
examples.vi: No, I am not. (used by both men and women)

examples.jp: ごはん[を]食べる？
あした京都[へ]行かない？
このりんご[は]おいしいね。
そこにはさみ[が]ある？
で, に, から, まで, と,
examples.vi: etc., however, are not omitted because the meaning of the sentence may not be clear without them.
In the plain style, いof Vて-form いるis also often dropped.

examples.jp: 辞書、持って[い]る？
examples.vi: Do you have a dictionary?

examples.jp: うん、持って[い]る。
examples.vi: Yes, I do.

examples.jp: ううん、持って[い]ない。
examples.vi: No, I don’t.

examples.jp: 5) けど
けどhas
examples.vi: the same function as が, which is used to connect two sentences (see Lesson 8, 7 and Lesson 14, 7). It is often used in conversations.

examples.jp: このカレーライス[は]おいしい？
うん、辛いけど、おいしい。
相撲のチケット[が]あるけど、いっしょに行かない？
いいね。

21.explanation:
1) When expressing conjecture

I think it will rain tomorrow.

I think Teresa has already gone to bed.

Does Mr. Miller know this news?

No, I don’t think he does.

2) When expressing one’s opinion

I think that prices are high in Japan.

What do you think of the new airport?

I think that it is clean but the access to it is not easy.

Agreement or disagreement with other people’s opinions can be expressed as follows.

A: Fax machines are convenient, aren’t they?

B: I think so, too. C: I don’t think so.

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: ideas or information expressed with おもいますare indicated by the particle と.

examples.jp: あした雨が降ると思います。
テレサちゃんはもう寝たと思います。
When
examples.vi: the content of conjecture is negative in nature, make the sentence before とnegative.

examples.jp: ミラーさんはこのニュースを知っていますか。
いいえ、たぶん知らないと思います。
日本は物価が高いと思います。
The
examples.vi: expression ～についてどうおもいますかis used to ask someone’s opinion on something by usingおもいます.と is not necessary afterどう.

examples.jp: 新しい空港についてどう思いますか。
きれいですが、ちょっと交通が不便だと思います。
A:ファクスは便利ですね。
B:わたしもそう思います。
C:わたしはそう[は]思いません。

21.meaning: 1) When quoting directly what someone says or said, repeat exactly what they say as in the following structure.
21.explanation:
1) When quoting directly what someone says or said, repeat exactly what they say as in the following structure.

We say “Good night” before going to bed.

Mr. Miller said “I will go to Tokyo on a business trip next week”.

Mr. Miller said that he would go to Tokyo on a business trip next week.

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: content expressed withいいます is indicated by the particle と.

examples.jp: 寝る前に「お休みなさい」と言います。
ミラーさんは「来週東京へ出張します」と言いました。

examples.vi: When quoting indirectly what someone says or said, the plain form is used before と. The tense of the quoted sentence is not affected by the tense of the main sentence.

examples.jp: ミラーさんは来週東京へ出張すると言いました。

21.meaning: You are going to the party tomorrow, aren’t you?
21.explanation:
You are going to the party tomorrow, aren’t you?

It was cold in Hokkaido, wasn’t it?

No, it wasn’t that cold.

examples.jp: な-adj /
examples.vi: N：～だ

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: the speaker expects that the listener has some knowledge on the topic being discussed and that the listener will agree with the speaker’s view,でしょう is said with a rising intonation to confirm the listener’s agreement.

examples.jp: あしたパーティーに行くでしょう？
ええ、行きます。
北海道は寒かったでしょう？
いいえ、そんなに寒くなったです。

21.meaning: A football game between Japan and Brazil will be held in Tokyo.
21.explanation: A football game between Japan and Brazil will be held in Tokyo.

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: N2 expresses such events as a party, concert, festival, incident, disaster and so on, ありますmeans “to take place” or “to be held”.

examples.jp: 東京で日本とブラジルのサッカーの試合があります。

21.meaning: Did you give your opinion at the meeting?
21.explanation: Did you give your opinion at the meeting?

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: some action takes place on a certain occasion, that occasion is followed by で.

examples.jp: 会議で何か意見を言いましたか。

21.meaning: Shall we drink beer or something?
21.explanation: Shall we drink beer or something?

examples.jp: でもis
examples.vi: used to give an example out of things of the same kind when one encourages or advises someone to do something or when one makes a suggestion.

examples.jp: ちょっとビールでも飲みませんか。

21.meaning: I have to go home now.
21.explanation: I have to go home now.

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: expression is made by omitting いけませんfrom Vない-formないといけません. V-ないformないといけません is similar to Vない-formなければなりませんwhich you learned in Lesson 17.

examples.jp: もう帰らないと。

22.meaning: You learned how to modify nouns in Lesson 2 and Lesson 8.
22.explanation:
You learned how to modify nouns in Lesson 2 and Lesson 8.

In Japanese, whatever modifies a word, whether it’s a word or a sentence, it always comes before the word to be modified. Here you learn another way to modify nouns.

examples.jp: ミラーさんのうち
examples.vi: Mr. Miller’s house (Lesson 2)

examples.jp: 新しいうち
examples.vi: a new house (Lesson 8)

examples.jp: きれいなうち
examples.vi: a beautiful house (Lesson 8)

22.meaning: a person who is kind and pretty
22.explanation:
a person who is kind and pretty

a person who is 65 years old.

2) Nouns, which are various elements of the sentence, are picked out of it and can be modified by it.

I saw a movie last week → the movie that I saw last week

Mr. Wang works at a hospital → the hospital where Mr. Wang works

I will meet a friend tomorrow → the friend whom I will meet tomorrow

3) The noun modified by a sentence (“the house where Mr. Miller lived” in the example sentences below) can be used in various parts of a sentence.

This is the house where Mr. Miller lived.

The house where Mr. Miller lived is old.

I bought the house where Mr. Miller lived.

I like the house where Mr. Miller lived.

There was a cat in the house where Mr. Miller lived.

I have been to the house where Mr. Miller lived.
