examples.vi: The predicate of the sentence which modifies a noun is in the plain form. In the case of な-adjective sentences, ～だ becomes ～な. In the case of noun sentences ～だ becomes ～の.

examples.jp: 行かない人
行った人
行かなかった人
親切で、きれいな人
65 歳の人
examples.vi: a person who is tall and has black hair

examples.jp: わたしは先週映画を見ました → わたしが先週見た映画
ワンさんは病院で働いています → ワンさんが働いている病院
わたしはあした友達に会います → わたしがあした会う友達
When
examples.vi: the nouns in three sentences above are modified, the particle を, でandに attached to them respectively are unnecessary.

examples.jp: これはミラーさんが住んでいたうちです。
ミラーさんが住んでいたうちは古いです。
ミラーさんが住んでいたうちを買いました。
わたしはミラーさんが住んでいたうちが好きです。
ミラーさんが住んでいたうちに猫がいました。
ミラーさんが住んでいたうちへ行ったことがあります。

22.meaning: Mr. Miller baked a cake. → This is the cake which Mr. Miller baked.
22.explanation:
Mr. Miller baked a cake. → This is the cake which Mr. Miller baked.

I like the picture that Ms. Karina drew.

Do you know the place where he was born?

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: a sentence modifies a noun, the subject in the sentence is indicated by が.

examples.jp: ミラーさんはケーキを作りました → これはミラーさんが作ったケーキです。
わたしはカリナさんがかいた絵が好きです。

22.pattern: V dictionary form 時間／約束／用事
22.meaning: I have no time to eat breakfast.
22.explanation:
I have no time to eat breakfast.

I have an arrangement to see a movie with a friend of mine.

I have something to do at the city hall today.

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: expressing the time for doing some activity, put the dictionary form of the action before じかん.

examples.jp: わたしは朝ごはんを食べる時間がありません。
You
examples.vi: can also say the content of the arrangement (appointment), etc., by putting the dictionary form of that action before やくそく, etc.

examples.jp: わたしは友達と映画を見る約束があります。
きょうは市役所へ行く用事があります。

23.meaning: When you borrow books from the library, you need a card.
23.explanation:
When you borrow books from the library, you need a card.

When you don’t know how to use it, ask me.

When I’m not in good shape, I drink “Genki-cha”.

Won’t you come to my place when you are free?

When my wife is sick, I take a day off work.

When I was young, I did not study much.

I used to swim in a river when I was a child.

examples.jp: ときconnects
examples.vi: two sentences and expresses the time when the state or action described in the main sentence exists or occurs. As shown in the table above, the forms of verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives and nouns connected to ときare the same as the forms when modifying nouns.

examples.jp: 図書館で本を借りるとき、カードが要ります。
使い方がわからないとき、わたしに聞いてください。
体の調子が悪いとき、「元気茶」を飲みます。
暇なとき、うちへ遊びに来ませんか。
妻が病気のとき、会社を休みます。
若いとき、あまり勉強しませんでした。
子どものとき、よく川で泳ぎました。
The
examples.vi: tense of adjective sentences and noun sentences which modify ときis not affected by the tense of the main sentence.

23.meaning: I bought a bag when I went back to my country.
23.explanation:
I bought a bag when I went back to my country.

I bought a bag when I went back to my country.

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: the dictionary form of the predicate is put beforeとき it indicates the non-completion of the action, and when the た-form of the predicate is put before ときin indicates the completion of the action.

examples.jp: 国へ帰るとき、かばんを買いました。(*)
国へ帰ったとき、かばんを買いました。(**)
In (*), かえるindicates
examples.vi: that at the time being referred to the action had not been completed, that the speaker had not reached his/her country yet and that he/she bought a bag somewhere on his/her way there (Japan is included). In (**), かえったindicates that the action was completed and the speaker bought a bag after arriving in his/her country.

23.explanation:
…, then (inevitably) …

Press this button, and the change will come out.

Turn this, and the volume will go up.

Turn to the right, and you will find the post office.

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: expressing the situation where, as a result of a certain action, another action or matter inevitably happens, とis used to connect the sentences.

examples.jp: このボタンを押すと、お釣りが出ます。
これを回すと、音が大きくなります。
右へ曲がると、郵便局があります。
Expressions
examples.vi: of one’s will, hope, invitation or request cannot be used in the sentence which follows ～と.

examples.jp: 映画を見に行きたいです(hope)
映画を見に行きませんか (invitation)
ちょっと手伝ってください (request)
In
examples.vi: those cases, the conditional expression ～たら is used instead of ～と (see Lesson 25).

23.explanation:
The volume is low.

The light became brighter.

Press this button, and a ticket will come out.

examples.jp: You
examples.vi: learned in Lesson 14 that the subject is indicated by がwhen describing a natural phenomenon. When describing a state or a scene as it is, the subject is also indicated by が.

examples.jp: 音が小さいです。
電気が明るくなります。
このボタンを押すと、切符が出ます。

23.meaning: I take a walk in the park.
23.explanation:
I take a walk in the park.

I cross the road.

I turn to the right at the intersection.

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: particle をis used to denote the place where a person or a thing passes. The verbs of movement such as さんぽします, わたります, あるきます, etc. are used in this pattern.

examples.jp: 公園を散歩します。
道を渡ります。
交差点を右へ曲がります。

24.meaning: I gave flowers to Ms. Sato.
24.explanation:
I gave flowers to Ms. Sato.

Ms. Sato gave me a Christmas card.

Ms. Sato gave candies to my younger sister.

examples.jp: You
examples.vi: learned that あげますmeans “give” in Lesson 7. This verb cannot be used when somebody else gives something to the speaker or the speaker’s family, etc. (x さとうさんはわたしにクリスマスカードをあげました). In this caseくれます is used.

examples.jp: わたしは佐藤さんに花をあげました。
佐藤さんはわたしにクリスマスカードをくれました。
佐藤さんは妹にお菓子をくれました。

24.meaning: I lent Ms. Kimura a book.
24.explanation:
I lent Ms. Kimura a book.

Shall I call a taxi for you?

Mr. Yamada told me the telephone number of the library.

This expression conveys a sense of gratitude on the part of those who receive a favor.

My mother sent me a sweater.

examples.jp: あげます, もらいますand くれますare
examples.vi: also used to refer to the giving and receiving of actions as well as those of things. They indicate who is doing that act for whom, while also expressing a sense of goodwill or gratitude. In this case, the act is expressed by the て-form.
Vて-formあげます

examples.jp: Vて-formあげます
examples.vi: indicates that one does something for somebody with a sense of goodwill.

examples.jp: わたしは木村さんに本を貸してあげました。
When
examples.vi: the speaker is the actor and the listener is the receiver of the act, this expression could give the impression that the speaker is being patronizing. You are, therefore, advised to avoid using this expression directly to someone whom you do not know very well or who is senior or superior to you. You may use it to someone with whom you have a very close, friendly relationship. So, when you offer assistance to someone who is not very close, Vます-form ましょうか (see Lesson 14,6) is used.

examples.jp: タクシーを呼びましょうか。
手伝いましょうか。

examples.vi: Vて-formもらいます

examples.jp: わたしは山田さんに図書館の電話番号を教えてもらいました。

examples.vi: Vて-formくれます

examples.jp: 母は[わたし]にセーターを送ってくれました。
Like
examples.vi: Vて-formもらいます, this expression also conveys a sense of gratitude on the part of those who receive a favor. The difference is that Vて-formもらいます has the receiver of the act as the subject of the sentence, while Vて-formくれます has the actor as the subject of the sentence, implying the actor (the subject) voluntarily takes the action. The receiver of the act in the latter case is often the speaker and わたし(the receiver) に is often omitted.

24.meaning: That’s a nice tie, isn’t it?
24.explanation:
That’s a nice tie, isn’t it?

Yes. Ms. Sato gave it to me.

examples.jp: すてきなネクタイですね。
ええ、佐藤さんがくれました。
You
examples.vi: present a topic, saying すてきなネクタイですね. Responding to it, your partner in conversation gives a piece of information on the topic which is unknown to you, [このネクタイは]さとうさんがくれました. The subject of the sentence giving new information is indicated by が.

24.meaning: Who will go to give him a hand?
24.explanation:
Who will go to give him a hand?

Ms. Karina will.

examples.jp: You
examples.vi: learned that when the subject is questioned, it is indicated byが in あります／いますsentences (Lesson 10) and adjective sentences (Lesson 12). This is also the case for verb sentences.

examples.jp: だれが手伝いに行きますか。
カリナさんが行きます。

25.meaning: If I had money, I would travel.
25.explanation:
If I had money, I would travel.

If I don’t have time, I will not watch TV.

If it’s inexpensive, I want to buy a personal computer.

If you are free, please give me a hand.

If it’s fine, won’t you take a walk with me?

examples.jp: When らis
examples.vi: attached to the past tense plain form of verbs, adjectives, etc., it changes the preceding clause into a conditional expression. When a speaker wants to state his opinion, situation, request, etc., in the conditional, this pattern is used.

examples.jp: お金があったら、旅行します。
時間がなかったら、テレビを見ません。
安かったら、パソコンを買いたいです。
暇だったら、手伝ってください。
いい天気だったら、散歩しませんか。

25.meaning: This pattern is used to express that a certain action will be done or a certain situation will appear when a matter, action or state which is sure to happen in the future has been completed or achieve
25.explanation:
This pattern is used to express that a certain action will be done or a certain situation will appear when a matter, action or state which is sure to happen in the future has been completed or achieved. The main sentence is always in the present tense.

Let’s go out when it gets to ten.

I take a shower soon after I return home.

examples.jp: １０時になったら、出かけましょう。
うちへ帰ったら、すぐシャワーを浴びます。

25.meaning: Even if it rains, I’ll do the laundry.
25.explanation:
Even if it rains, I’ll do the laundry.

Even if group tours are expensive, I don’t like them.

Even if a personal computer is useful, I won’t use it.

Even if it is Sunday, I will work.

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: expression is used to present a reverse condition. Contrary to plain past form ら、～, this expression is used when an action which is expected to be taken or an event which is expected to happen naturally under the given circumstances does not materialize or a thing turns out in a way opposite to a socially accepted idea.

examples.jp: 雨が降っても、洗濯します。
安くても、わたしはグループ旅行が嫌いです。
便利でも、パソコンを使いません。
日曜日でも、働きます。

25.meaning: If I had 100 million yen, I would want to travel to various countries.
25.explanation:
If I had 100 million yen, I would want to travel to various countries.

No matter how much I think, I can’t understand this.

No matter how expensive it is, I will buy it.

examples.jp: もしis
examples.vi: used in a sentence in the plain past form to indicate beforehand that the sentence is going to present a condition, while いくらis used with ～ても（～でも）to do the same. もしimplies that an emphasis is on the speaker’s supposition while いくらis meant to stress the degree of conditionality.

examples.jp: もし一億円あったら、いろいろな国を旅行したいです。
いくら考えても、わかりません。
いくら高くても、買います。

25.meaning: I will clean my room before my friends come.
25.explanation:
I will clean my room before my friends come.

When my wife is sick, I take a day off work.

If your friend doesn’t come on time, what will you do?

examples.jp: As
examples.vi: mentioned in Lesson 16, 4. [Note], the subject of a subordinate clause is indicated by が. In subordinate clause using たら, ても, とき, と, まえに, etc., in addition to から, the subject is indicated by が, as shown below.

examples.jp: 友達が来るまえに、部屋を掃除します。
妻が病気のとき、会社を休みます。
友達が約束の時間に来なかったら、どうしますか。

26.meaning: This expression is used in the following cases.
26.explanation:
This expression is used in the following cases.

Ms. Watanabe, you sometimes speak Osaka dialect.

Have you lived in Osaka?

Yes, I lived in Osaka until I was fifteen.

The design of your shoes is interesting. Where did you buy them?

I bought this pair at Edoya Store .

Why were you late?

What’s the matter?

This expression is used in the following cases.

Why were you late?

Because the bus didn’t come.

What’s the matter?

I don’t feel well.

Do you read newspaper every morning?

No. I don’t have the time.

I’ve written a letter in Japanese. Could you please check it for me?

I want to tour NHK. How can I do that?

examples.jp: な-adj /
examples.vi: N: ～だ → ～な

examples.jp: ～んですis
examples.vi: an expression used to explain cause, reasons, grounds, etc., strongly . ～んですis used in spoken language, while ～のです is used in written language. ～んですis used in the following ways.

examples.jp: 1) ～んですか
渡辺さんは時々大阪弁を使いますね。
大阪に住んでいたんですか。
ええ、１５歳まで大阪に住んでいました。
おもしろいデザインの靴です。どこで買ったんですか。
エドヤストアで買いました。
どうして遅れたんですか。(*)
どうしたんですか。(**)
2) ～んです
どうして遅れたんですか。
バスが来なかったんです。
どうしたんですか。
ちょっと気分が悪いんです。
毎朝新聞を読みますか。
いいえ。時間がないんです。
3) ～んですが、～
～んですがis
examples.vi: used to introduce a topic. It is followed by a request, an invitation or an expression seeking permission. がin this case is used to connect sentences lightly and indicates hesistation and reservation on the speaker’s side.

examples.jp: 日本語で手紙を書いたんですが、ちょっと見ていただけませんか。
NHK を見学したいんですが、どうしたらいいですか。

26.meaning: Would you please do me a favor of ~ing?
26.explanation:
Would you please do me a favor of ~ing?

Would you please introduce a good teacher to me?

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: is a request expression that is politer than ～てください

examples.jp: いい先生を紹介していただけませんか。

26.explanation:
What/When/Where/Which/How/Who shall I?

Where should I buy a camera?

I don’t have any small change. What shall I do?

examples.jp: どこでカメラを買ったらいいですか。
細かいお金がないんですが、どうしたらいいですか。
～たらいいですかis
examples.vi: used when the speaker asks the listener for some advice or instructions about what to do.

26.pattern: N (object) は好きです／嫌いです／上手です／下手です／あります
26.meaning: like / dislike / be good at / be poor at / have, etc. N
26.explanation:
like / dislike / be good at / be poor at / have, etc. N

Are you going to participate in the athletic meeting?

No. I don’t like sports very much.

examples.jp: 運動会に参加しますか。
いいえ。スポーツはあまり好きじゃないんです。
You
examples.vi: learned in Book I (Lesson 10 and 17) that subjects and also objects indicated by をcan become topics and be highlighted byは . Objects indicated by がcan also be highlighted byは .

examples.jp: かいます かけます
かえます かける
かえる
examples.vi: II たべます たべられます たべられる III きます

examples.jp: します こられます
できます こられる
できる
All
examples.vi: potential verbs are Group II verbs; they conjugate into the dictionary form, ない-form, て-form, etc.

examples.jp: eg. かける、かけ（ない）、かけて
わかる,
examples.vi: which includes the meaning of possibility itself, does not change into わかれる.

27.explanation:
I speak Japanese.

I can speak Japanese.

Can you go to the hospital alone?

I could not see Mr. Tanaka.

2) Potential verbs have two uses: to express a person’s ability to do something and to express the possibility of an action in a certain situation.

Mr. Miller can read Kanji.

You can change dollars at this bank.

examples.vi: Potential verbs express not actions but states. The object of transitive verbs are indicated by the particle を. When they become potential verbs, however, their objects are usually indicated by the particle が.

examples.jp: わたしは日本語を話します。
わたしは日本語が話せます。
No
examples.vi: particles change except for を.

examples.jp: 一人で病院へ行けますか。
田中さんに会えませんでした。
ミラーさんは漢字が読めます。
この銀行でドルが換えられます。

27.pattern: 見えます and 聞こえます.
27.meaning: You can now see Kurosawa’s movies in Shinjuku.
27.explanation:
You can now see Kurosawa’s movies in Shinjuku.

Mt. Fuji can be seen from the Shinkansen.

You can hear the weather forecast by phone.

The sound of a radio can be heard.

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: potential verb forみます is みられますand that forききます isきけます. They indicate that one’s intention of seeing or listening can be realized. みえますindicates that a certain object comes into one’s sight and きこえますthat a sound reaches one’s ears independent of one’s will. In sentences using みえますandきこえます , objects to be seen or heard become the objects of the sentence and are indicated by が.

examples.jp: 新宿でいま黒沢の映画が見られます。
新幹線から富士山が見えます。
電話で天気予報が聞けます。
ラジオの音がが聞こえます。

27.meaning: The verb that you learn here means “come into being”, ‘come up”, “be completed”, “be finished”, “be made”, etc.
27.explanation:
The verb that you learn here means “come into being”, ‘come up”, “be completed”, “be finished”, “be made”, etc.

A big supermarket has been completed in front of the station.

When can you fix this watch?

examples.jp: 駅の前に大きいスーパーができました。
時計の修理はいつできますか。

27.meaning: In my school there is an American teacher.
27.explanation:
In my school there is an American teacher.

In my school we can learn the Chinese language.

Yesterday we could see mountains, but not today.

I drink wine, but not beer.

I will go to Tokyo, but not to Osaka.

examples.jp: 1) では／には／へは／からは／までは、etc.
はis
examples.vi: used to highlight a noun as a topic, and as you learned in Lesson 10, 17 and 26, when がorを follows the noun, it is replaced byは . When other particles (e.g.で、に、へ、 etc.) follow the noun, はis placed after them.

examples.jp: わたしの学校にはアメリカ人の先生がいます。
わたしの学校では中国語が習えます。
2) はused
examples.vi: to make contrasts

examples.jp: きのうは山が見えましたが、きょうは見えません。
ワインは飲みますが、ビールは飲みません。
京都へは行きますが、大阪へは行きません。

27.meaning: Klara can speak English. She can speak French, too.
27.explanation:
Klara can speak English. She can speak French, too.

I went to America last year. I went to Mexico, too.

The sea can be seen from my room, and also from my brother’s room.

examples.jp: Like
examples.vi: the usage ofは explained above, もreplaces をor が, but follows other particles. In the case of the particleへ , however, it can be omitted.

examples.jp: クララさんは英語が話せます。フランス語も話せます。
去年アメリカへ行きました。メキシコ[へ]も行きました。
わたしの部屋から海が見えます。弟の部屋からも見えます。

27.meaning: I cannot write anything but Roman letters.
27.explanation:
I cannot write anything but Roman letters.

I can only write Roman letters.

examples.jp: しかis
examples.vi: attached to nouns, quantifiers, etc., and is always used with negative predicates. It highlights the words, makes limitations and negates everything except the thing expressed by the word. It replacesが orを, but follows other particles. しかhas a negative nuance, while だけhas a positive one.

examples.jp: ローマ字しか書けません。
ローマ字だけ書けます。

28.pattern: V1 ます-form ながら V2
28.meaning: This sentence pattern means that one person does two different actions (V1 and V2) at the same time. The action denoted by V2 is the more emphasized of the two actions.
28.explanation:
This sentence pattern means that one person does two different actions (V1 and V2) at the same time. The action denoted by V2 is the more emphasized of the two actions.

I listen to music while eating.

This sentence pattern is also used when the two actions take place over a period of time.

I’m working and studying Japanese.

examples.jp: 音楽を聞きながら食事します。
働きながら日本語を勉強しています。

28.explanation:
I jog every morning.

I used to go to bed at eight every evening when I was a child.

examples.jp: An
examples.vi: individual’s customary action is expressed by this sentence pattern. A customary action in the past is expressed by using Vて-formいました.

examples.jp: 毎朝ジョギングをしています。
子どものとき、毎晩８時に寝ていました。

28.meaning: 1) When sentences are stated from a certain viewpoint, they can be linked using this structure. For example, sentences describing the merits of a particular subject are joined into one sentence using
28.explanation:
1) When sentences are stated from a certain viewpoint, they can be linked using this structure. For example, sentences describing the merits of a particular subject are joined into one sentence using this pattern.

Professor Watt is earnest and diligent and has experience.

2) This structure is also used to state causes or reasons when there is more than one cause or reason. Use of this structure implies the meaning of “furthermore” or “on top of that”.

This shop is very convenient. It’s near the station, and you can also come here by car.

When the speaker states reasons using this pattern, he/she sometimes does not say his/her conclusion when it’s understood from the context.

Could you teach English to my son?

Sorry. I often go on business trip, and I have to take a Japanese exam soon.

Because the color is beautiful (and for some other reasons), I’ll buy this pair of shoes.

examples.jp: ワット先生は熱心だし、まじめだし、経験もあります。
駅から近いし、車でも来られるし、この店はとても便利です。
息子に英語を教えていただけませんか。
うーん、出張も多いし、もうすぐ日本語の試験もあるし，…。
There
examples.vi: are cases when only one ～し is used in a sentence. Though the reason explicitly stated is only one, the existence of other reasons is implied by the use of ～し. Note this is not true of ～から.

examples.jp: 色もきれいだし、この靴を買います。
As
examples.vi: you seen above, the particleも is often used in this sentence pattern. By the use ofも , the speaker implies the existence of other reasons to emphasize his/her viewpoint.

28.meaning: Why did you choose Sakura University?
28.explanation:
Why did you choose Sakura University?

Because it’s my father’s alma mater, there are many good teachers, and besides, it’s near my house.

examples.jp: それにis
examples.vi: used when you add another similar fact or situation.

examples.jp: どうしてさくら大学を選んだんですか。
さくら大学は、父が出た大学し、いい先生も多いし、それに家から近いですから。

28.meaning: The food is inexpensive and tasty in this restaurant.
28.explanation:
The food is inexpensive and tasty in this restaurant.

And that’s why it’s crowded.

examples.jp: それでis
examples.vi: a conjunction used to show that what was said before it is the reason or cause for the sentence following it.

examples.jp: このレストランは値段も安いし、おいしいんです。
それで、人が多いんですね。

28.meaning: Do you often go to this coffee shop?
28.explanation: Do you often go to this coffee shop?

examples.jp: よくこの喫茶店に来るんですか。
In
examples.vi: this sentence, the particle に, which indicates one’s destination, is used instead of the particle へ, which shows direction. Verbs like いきます、きます、かえりますandしゅっちょうします are used with either “placeへ” or “placeに”.

29.explanation:
The window is broken.

The light is on.

The road was crowded this morning.

This chair is broken.

examples.jp: Vて-form いますexpresses
examples.vi: the state which results as a consequence of the action expressed by the verb.
N がVて-formいます

examples.jp: 窓が割れています。(*)
電気がついています。
As
examples.vi: in the examples above, when the speaker describes the state he/she sees in front of him/her as it is, the subject of the action is indicated with が. Example (*) shows that “the window was broken in the past and at present its consequence remains (=it is broken)”. Verbs which are used with this expression are intransitive verbs, and most of them indicate an instantaneous act or action. Examples of such verbs include こわれます、きえます、あきます、こみますetc.

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: describing states in the past, Vて-formいました is used.

examples.jp: けさは道が込んでいました。

examples.vi: N はV-てformいます

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: the subject of an act or action is taken up as a topic, it is indicated with は.

examples.jp: このいすは壊れています。

29.pattern: Vて-form しまいました／しまいます
29.meaning: We drank all the wine that Mr. Schmidt brought.
29.explanation:
We drank all the wine that Mr. Schmidt brought.

I have done my Kanji homework already.

I intend to finish writing the report by lunch time.

examples.jp: Vて-form しまいましたis
examples.vi: an expression which emphasizes that an action or event has been completed.

examples.jp: シュミットさんが持って来たワインは全部飲んでしまいました。
漢字の宿題はもうやってしまいました。
Though
examples.vi: V ましたcan also indicate the completion of an action, the use of Vて-form しまいました emphasizes the actual completion of the action. Therefore, the latter is often accompanied by such adverbs asもう and/or ぜんぶ .

examples.jp: Also,
examples.vi: Vて-formしまいますexpresses that the speaker will complete an action in the future.

examples.jp: 昼ごはんまでにレポートを書いてしまいます。

29.meaning: This expression conveys the speaker’s embarrassment or regret in a difficult situation.
29.explanation:
This expression conveys the speaker’s embarrassment or regret in a difficult situation.

I lost my passport.

My personal computer’s broken.

examples.jp: パスポートをなくしてしまいました。
パソコンが故障してしまいました。
Though
examples.vi: the fact of losing one’s passport or the trouble with the computer can be expressed with なくしましたor 故障しました, these sentences above add the speaker’s feeling of regret or embarrassment about the fact.

examples.jp: ありましたhere
examples.vi: is used to indicate that the speaker has found the bag, not to indicate that it existed some place in the past.

29.explanation:
I’ve lost my wallet somewhere.

Is there a pay phone somewhere nearby?

examples.jp: In
examples.vi: the examples shown in Lesson 13, you learned へin どこかへand をin なにかをcould be omitted. However, でin どこかでor にinどこかに cannot be omitted.

examples.jp: どこかで財布をなくしてしまいました。
どこかに電話がありませんか。

30.meaning: There is a memo put on the desk.
30.explanation:
There is a memo put on the desk.

This month’s schedule is written on the calendar.

In (*), it is indicated that someone put a memo on the desk (for later use) and as a result the memo exists as it was put. (**) indicates that someone wrote this month’s schedule on the calendar (so as not to forget it) and as a result the schedule is written on the calendar.

This sentence pattern is used when N2 in sentence pattern 1) is taken up as a topic.

Where is the memo?

It’s been put on the desk.

This month’s schedule has been written on the calendar.

I have already bought the present for her birthday. (i)

I have already reserved a hotel. (ii)

The window is closed. (x)

The window has been closed (for some purpose). (y)

examples.jp: Vて-formあります
examples.vi: indicates the state which results as a consequence of an action intentionally done by somebody. The verbs used here are transitive verbs that express intentions.
N1 にN2 がVて-form あります

examples.jp: 机の上にメモが置いてあります。(*)
カレンダーに今月の予定が書いてあります。(**)

examples.vi: N2は N1 Vて-formあります

examples.jp: メモはどこですか。
今月の予定はカレンダーに書いてあります。

examples.vi: Vて-formあります is used to express the state of the result of what somebody did for some purpose. So it can also express, as in (i) and (ii), that some preparation has been done. It is often used with もう.

examples.jp: 誕生日のプレゼントはもう買ってあります。
ホテルはもう予約してあります。
窓が閉まっています。
窓が閉めてあります。

examples.vi: simply describes the state that the window is closed, while (y) implies that somebody (it could be the speaker himself) closed the window with some objective or intention in mind. Most verbs used in Vて-form いますare intransitive, while verbs used in Vて-formあります are transitive.

30.explanation:
This sentence pattern means:

1) Completion of a necessary action by a given time

I’ll buy a ticket before the trip.

What should I do before the next meeting?

Read these materials.

2) Completion of a necessary action in readiness for the next use or the next step.

When you finish using the scissors, put them back where they were.

30) Keeping the resultant state as it is.

Please leave the chairs as they are because a meeting will be held here tomorrow.

examples.jp: 旅行のまえに切符を買っておきます。
次の会議までに何をしておいたらいいですか。
この資料を読んでおいてください。
はさみを使ったら、元の所に戻しておいてください。
あした会議がありますから、いすはこのままにしておいてください。

30.explanation:
It’s still raining.

Shall I put away the tools?

No, leave them there. I’m still using them.

examples.jp: まだ雨が降っています。
道具を片づけましょうか。
まだ使っていますから、そのままにしておいてください。
まだabove
examples.vi: means “as yet” or “still” and indicates that the action or the state is continuing.

30.meaning: I’d like to see a musical on Broadway…
30.explanation:
I’d like to see a musical on Broadway…

That sounds nice.

I will be transferred to the Osaka head office next month.

Congratulations!

Sometimes I have pains in my stomach and head.

examples.jp: ブロードウェイでミュージカルを見たいと思うんですが…。
それはいいですね。
来月から大阪の本社に転勤なんです。
それはおめでとうございます。
時々頭や胃が痛くなるんです。
それはいけませんね。
それabove
examples.vi: refers to what is stated just before.

31.meaning: The volitional form of verbs is made as follows.
31.explanation: The volitional form of verbs is made as follows.

examples.jp: Group
examples.vi: I: Replace the sound of the ます-form with the sound in the お-line of the same row and attach う.

examples.jp: Group
examples.vi: II: Attach ようto the ます-form.

examples.jp: Group
examples.vi: III: しますbecomesしよう , and きますbecomes こよう.

31.pattern: How to use the volitional form.
31.explanation:
1) In plain style sentences

Shall we take a rest?

How about taking a rest for a while?

Shall I help you?

I’m thinking of going to the beach at the weekend.

I’m going to the bank now.

He is thinking of working in a foreign country.

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: volitional form is used instead of ～しましょうin plain style sentences.

examples.jp: ちょっと休まない？
うん、休もう。
少し休もうか。
手伝おうか。

examples.vi: V volitional form と思っています

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: sentence pattern is used to express what the speaker is thinking of doing. V volitional form とおもいますcan also express a similar meaning. V volitional formとおもっています implies that the speaker’s decision was made some time ago.

examples.jp: 週末は海に行こうと思っています。
今から銀行へ行こうと思います。
彼は外国で働こうと思っています。

31.pattern: V dictionary form つもりです
31.meaning: V dictionary form つもりです

31.pattern: Vない-formない つもりです
31.meaning: Even when I go back to my country, I’ll continue with my judo.
31.explanation:
Even when I go back to my country, I’ll continue with my judo.

I’m determined not to smoke from tomorrow.

examples.jp: V
examples.vi: dictionary formつもりです is used to express the speaker’s intention of doing something, and Vない-formない つもりですis used to express the speaker’s intention of not doing something.

examples.jp: 国へ帰っても、柔道を続けるつもりです。
あしたからはタバコを吸わないつもりです。

31.meaning: By using this sentence pattern, you can inform people of schedules.
31.explanation:
By using this sentence pattern, you can inform people of schedules.

I’m scheduled to go on a business trip to Germany at the end of July.

The trip is scheduled to last for a week.

examples.jp: ７月の終わりにドイツへ出張する予定です。
旅行は１週間ぐらいの予定です。

31.meaning: This sentence pattern means that something has not taken place or has not been done yet.
31.explanation:
This sentence pattern means that something has not taken place or has not been done yet.

The bank is not open yet.

Have you written the report yet?

examples.jp: 銀行は、まだ開いていません。
レポートはもう書きましたか。
いいえ、まだ書いていません。

31.meaning: There’s one thing missing in Tokyo. And that’s beautiful nature.
31.explanation:
There’s one thing missing in Tokyo. And that’s beautiful nature.

What I want most is a “magic door”. When you open this door, you can go anywhere you want.

examples.jp: In
examples.vi: writing, a demonstrative belonging to the そ-series, is used when a word(s) or a sentence(s) mentioned before is referred to. Sometimes, however, a demonstrative belonging to the こ-series is used instead. In this case, the writer wants to describe something to the reader as though it really existed then and there.

examples.jp: 東京にない物が１つだけある。それは美しい自然だ。
わたしがいちばん欲しい物は[どこでもドア」です。このドアを開けると、どこでも行きたい所へ行けます。

32.pattern: Vない-formない ほうがいい
32.meaning: It is good to do some exercise everyday.
32.explanation:
It is good to do some exercise everyday.

I have got a fever.

So, you had better not take a bath.

This pattern is used to make suggestions or to give advice. Depending on the situation, this expression may sound like you are imposing your opinion on the listener. Therefore, consider the context of the conversation carefully before using it.

I would like to see some Japanese temples.

So, it would be a good idea to go to Kyoto.

examples.jp: 毎日運動したほうがいいです。
熱があるんです。
じゃ、おふろに入らないほうがいいですよ。
日本のお寺が見たいんですが…。(*)
じゃ、京都へ行ったらいいですよ。
Example (*)
examples.vi: illustrates a situation in which a simple suggestion is given. In such cases～たらいい is used. ～たほうがいいimplies a comparison and a choice between two things even if it is not expressed in words.

32.meaning: This pattern expresses the speaker’s inference from some information he/she has. When used in a question like (**), the speaker asks for the listener’s inference.
32.explanation:
This pattern expresses the speaker’s inference from some information he/she has. When used in a question like (**), the speaker asks for the listener’s inference.

It will rain tomorrow.

Do you think Mr. Thawaphon will pass the exam?

examples.jp: あしたは雨が降るでしょう。
タワポンさんは合格するでしょうか。(**)

32.meaning: We might not be in time for the appointment.
32.explanation: We might not be in time for the appointment.

examples.jp: ～かもしれませんalso
examples.vi: expresses the speaker’s inference, and means that there is a possibility that some event or state occurred/occurs/will occur. The degree of certainty however is much lower than with ～でしょう.

examples.jp: 約束の時間に間に合わないかもしれません。

32.pattern: きっと／たぶん／もしかしたら
32.explanation:
Mr. Miller will surely come.

It will undoubted rain tomorrow.

Do you think Mr. Miller will come?

I guess Mr. Yamada does not know this news.

There is a possibility I might be unable to graduate in March.

examples.jp: 1) きっと
This
examples.vi: adverb indicates that the speaker is quite certain of what he/she is saying. The probability ranges from quite high to same level as that of ～でしょう.

examples.jp: ミラーさんはきっと来ます。
あしたはきっと雨でしょう。
2) たぶん
This
examples.vi: adverb entails less certainty thanきっと, and is mostly used with～でしょう . As shown below, たぶんis very often used with とおもいます.

examples.jp: ミラーさんは来るでしょうか。
たぶん来るでしょう。
山田さんはこのニュースをたぶん知らないと思います。
3) もしかしたら
This
examples.vi: adverb is used with～かもしれません in most cases. A sentence withもしかしたら expresses the idea that there is less of a possibility of some event or situation occurring.

examples.jp: もしかしたら３月に卒業できないかもしれません。

32.explanation:
Is anything bothering you?

I want to go skiing. Could you recommend a good ski resort?

examples.jp: 何か心配なことがありますか。(***)
As
examples.vi: shown in (***), you cannot say しんぱいな なにか, but you should rather say なにか しぱいなこと. Other similar examples are なにか～もの, どこか～ところ, だれか～ひと, いつか～とき, etc.

examples.jp: スキーに行きたいんですが、どこかいい所ありませんか。

32.meaning: Can I reach the station in thirty minutes?
32.explanation:
Can I reach the station in thirty minutes?

Can I buy a video player for 30,000 yen?

examples.jp: でadded
examples.vi: to a quantifier indicates the limit of a price, time, quantity, etc., necessary for a state, an action or an event to be realized.

examples.jp: 駅まで３０分で行けますか。
３万円でビデオが買えますか。

33.meaning: 1) How to make imperative form of verbs
33.explanation:
1) How to make imperative form of verbs

2) How to make the prohibitive form of verbs

examples.jp: Group
examples.vi: I: Change the last sound of the ます-form into the sound of the え-line.

examples.jp: Group
examples.vi: II: Attach ろto the ます-form.

examples.jp: Group
examples.vi: III: しますbecomesしろ, and きますbecomes こい.

examples.jp: With
examples.vi: every verb, attach なto the dictionary form.

33.pattern: Use of the imperative and prohibitive forms.
33.meaning: 1) The imperative form is used to force a person to do something and the prohibitive form is used to command a person not to do something. Both the imperative and prohibitive forms have strong coerciv
33.explanation:
1) The imperative form is used to force a person to do something and the prohibitive form is used to command a person not to do something. Both the imperative and prohibitive forms have strong coercive connotations, so much so that the use of these forms alone or at the end of an imperative sentence is very limited. In colloquial expressions, the use of either form is, in most cases, limited to male speakers.

2) Both the imperative and prohibitive forms are used alone or at the end of a sentence in the following instances:

– By a man senior in status or age to a person junior to him, or by a father to his child.

– When there is not enough time to be very polite; e.g., when giving instructions to a large number of people in a factory or during an emergency, etc. Even in this case, they may be used only by men senior in status or age.

– When a command is required during training many people or making students take exercise at schools and sport clubs.

– When cheering at sporting events. In this case the expressions below are sometimes used by women as well.

– When a strong impact or brevity is required, as in traffic sign or in slogan.

examples.jp: 早く寝ろ。
examples.vi: Go to bed immediately.

examples.jp: 遅れるな。
examples.vi: Don’t be late.
Between men who are friends. In this case, the particleよ is often attached at the end of the sentence to soften the tone.

examples.jp: あしたうちへ来い[よ]。
examples.vi: Come to my house tomorrow.

examples.jp: あまり飲むな[よ]。
examples.vi: Don’t drink too much.

examples.jp: 逃げろ。
examples.vi: Run.

examples.jp: エレベーターを使うな。
examples.vi: Don’t use the elevator.

examples.jp: 休め。
examples.vi: Rest.

examples.jp: 休むな。
examples.vi: Don’t rest.

examples.jp: 頑張れ。
examples.vi: Fight!

examples.jp: 負けるな。
examples.vi: Don’t lose!

examples.jp: 止まれ。
examples.vi: Stop.

examples.jp: 入るな。
examples.vi: Don’t enter.

examples.jp: 勉強しなさい。
examples.vi: Study.

33.pattern: ～と読みますand～と書いてあります
33.meaning: How do you read that Kanji?
33.explanation:
How do you read that Kanji?

“Tomare” is written over there.

examples.jp: あの漢字は何と読むんですか。
あそこに「止まれ｣と書いてあります。
とabove
examples.vi: indicates the content in the same way as とof～といいます (Lesson 21).

33.pattern: X はY という意味です
33.explanation:
“Tachiiri-Kinshi” means don’t enter.

What does this sign mean?

It means machine washable.

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: pattern is used to define the meaning of the word represented by “X” .という comes from といいます. The interrogativeどういう is used to ask the meaning.

examples.jp: 「立入禁止」は入るなという意味です。
このマークはどういう意味ですか。
洗濯機で洗えるという意味です。

33.pattern: “ S “ と言っていました
33.meaning: “ S “ と言っていました

33.pattern: plain formと言っていました
33.meaning: Mr. Tanaka said, “I will take a day off work tomorrow.”
33.explanation:
Mr. Tanaka said, “I will take a day off work tomorrow.”

Mr. Tanaka said that he would take a day off work tomorrow.

examples.jp: ～といいましたis
examples.vi: used when quoting a third person’s words (Lesson 21), while ～といっていましたis used when conveying a third person’s message.

examples.jp: 田中さんは「あした休みました」と言っていました。
田中さんはあした休むと言っていました。

33.pattern: “ S “ と伝えていただけませんか
33.meaning: “ S “ と伝えていただけませんか

33.pattern: plain formと伝えていただけませんか
33.meaning: These expressions are used when politely asking someone to convey a message.
33.explanation:
These expressions are used when politely asking someone to convey a message.

Could you please tell Mr. Wang to give me a call later?

Could you please tell Ms. Watanabe that the party tomorrow will be from 6 o’clock?

examples.jp: ワンさんに「あとで電話をください」と伝えていただけませんか。
すみませんが、渡辺さんにあしたのパーティーは６時からだと伝えていただけませんか。

34.pattern: V1 dictionary form / V1た-form / Nの +とおりに、V2
34.meaning: This means to copy exactly in words or actions (V2) what one has heard, seen, read or learnt, etc. (V1).
34.explanation:
This means to copy exactly in words or actions (V2) what one has heard, seen, read or learnt, etc. (V1).

Please do as I do.

Please write down what I say as it is.

Please tell us what you saw as it was.

This means that an action (V) is done in accordance with the manner shown by the preceding phrase.

Please cut the paper following the line.

I assembled it according to the handbook.

examples.vi: V1とおりに、V2

examples.jp: わたしがやるとおりに、やってください。
わたしが言うとおりに、書いてください。
見たとおりに、話してください。
The
examples.vi: dictionary form is used when the action denoted by V1 is going to be done from now, and the た-form is used when the action denoted by V1 has already been done.
Nのとおりに、 V

examples.jp: 線のとおりに、紙を切ってください。
説明書のとおりに、組み立てました。

34.meaning: This sentence pattern means the action or occurrence denoted by V2 takes place after the action or occurrence denoted by V1 or N has taken place.
34.explanation:
This sentence pattern means the action or occurrence denoted by V2 takes place after the action or occurrence denoted by V1 or N has taken place.

After I bought a new watch, I found the one I’d lost.

Shall we go and have a drink after work?

examples.jp: 新しいのを買ったあとで、なくした時計が見つかりました。
仕事のあとで、飲みに行きませんか。
Compared
examples.vi: with Vて-formから, which has a similar meaning, Vた-form あとでemphasizes the time context in which the respective events happen.

34.pattern: V1て-form / V1ない-formないで + V2
34.meaning: V1 is an action or condition which accompanies the action denoted by V2.
34.explanation:
V1 is an action or condition which accompanies the action denoted by V2.

We eat it with soy sauce.

We eat it without soy sauce.

examples.jp: しょうゆをつけて食べます。
しょうゆをつけないで食べます。

34.meaning: This pattern is used when the speaker indicates a course of action taken out of two alternative possibilities presented.
34.explanation:
This pattern is used when the speaker indicates a course of action taken out of two alternative possibilities presented.

Next Sunday I won’t go anywhere. I will rest at home instead.

examples.jp: 日曜日はどこも行かないで、うちでゆっくり休みます。

35.pattern: How to make the conditional form
35.meaning: How to make the conditional form

examples.jp: Group
examples.vi: I: Change the last sound of the ます-form into the sound of the え-line and attach ば.

examples.jp: Group
examples.vi: II: Attach ればto the ます-form.

examples.jp: Group
examples.vi: III: きますbecomesくれば, andします becomesすれば.

examples.jp: い-adj:
examples.vi: Change the last いof the い-adjectives into ければ.

examples.jp: な-adj:
examples.vi: Delete the last なof the な-adjectives and attach なら.

examples.jp: Noun:
examples.vi: Attach ならto the noun.

35.meaning: With the use of the conditional form, the former part of the sentence describes the requirements needed for an event or occurrence to manifest itself.
35.explanation:
With the use of the conditional form, the former part of the sentence describes the requirements needed for an event or occurrence to manifest itself.

When the subject of the former part is the same as that of the latter, you cannot use verbs containing volition in both parts of the sentence at the same time.

1) When describing the requirements needed for a certain event to manifest itself:

If you push the button, the window will open.

If he goes, I will go, too.

When it is fine, an island can be seen over there.

2) When describing the speaker’s judgement on what the other person has said or the situation:

If you don’t have any other opinions, let’s close this meeting now.

Do I have to hand in the report by tomorrow?

If it is possible, hand it in by this Friday.

Press here, and the door will open.

If you press here, the door will open.

If I don’t have time, I will not watch television.

If I don’t have time, I will not watch television.

examples.jp: ボタンを押せば、窓が開きます。
彼が行けば、わたしも行きます。
いい天気なら、向こうに島が見えます。
ほかに意見がなければ、これで終わりましょう。
あしたまでにレポートを出さなければなりませんか。
無理なら、金曜日までに出してください。
～とis
examples.vi: used when the result derived from the action described before とis inevitable. It is a predictable event or an unavoidable fact. ～とcannot not be used when it comes to the speaker’s wishes, judgement, permission, hopes, requests, etc.

examples.jp: ここを押すと、ドアが開きます。
We
examples.vi: can also use ～ば.

examples.jp: ここを押せば、ドアが開きます。
As
examples.vi: you learned in Lesson 25, ～たらhas two usages: (1) it is a conditional expression, and (2) it indicates that an action or state will occur or appear when certain conditions have been met.

examples.jp: 時間がなかったら、テレビを見ません。(*)
時間がなければ、テレビを見ません。(**)
ｘ 時間がないと、テレビを見ません。
東京へ来たら、ぜひ連絡してください。(***)
ｘ 東京へ来れば、ぜひ連絡してください。
In (*)
examples.vi: and (**), ～たらand ～ばcan be used, as unlike ～と, they are followed by expressions reflecting the speaker’s will. In (***), however, only ～たらmay be used because the subjects in the former and the latter denote the same person and both verbs are volitional. Although ～たらhas the widest range of use, as is shown above, it is not usually used in newspaper articles and business reports because it is colloquial.

35.meaning: I want to visit a hot spring resort. Don’t you know any good place?
35.explanation:
I want to visit a hot spring resort. Don’t you know any good place?

If you are talking about hot springs, Hakuba may be good.

examples.jp: N ならis
examples.vi: also used when the speaker takes up the topic introduced by the other person and gives some information on it.

examples.jp: 温泉に行きたいんですが、どこかいい所ありませんか。
温泉なら、白馬がいいですよ。

35.pattern: Interrogative V conditional form いいですか
35.meaning: I would like to borrow some books. What should I do?
35.explanation:
I would like to borrow some books. What should I do?

I would like to borrow some books. What should I do?

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: expression is used to ask for directions or suggestions as to the best course of action. It is used in the same way as ～たらいいですかthat you learned in Lesson 26.

examples.jp: 本を借りたいんですが、どうすればいいですか。
本を借りたいんですが、どうしたらいいですか。

35.meaning: The more I listen to the music of the Beatles, the better I like it.
35.explanation:
The more I listen to the music of the Beatles, the better I like it.

The easier of the operation, the better the computer

examples.jp: ビートルズの音楽は聞けば聞くほど好きになります。
パソコンは操作が簡単なら簡単なほどいいです。
The
examples.vi: same verb or adjective is used in front of both ば/ならand ほどin the same sentence. This pattern expresses that the degree described in the latter part of the sentence increases/decreases with the change of the degree described in the former.

36.meaning: V1 indicates a purpose or an aim, while V2 indicates a volitional action to get closer to that objective.
36.explanation:
V1 indicates a purpose or an aim, while V2 indicates a volitional action to get closer to that objective.

I practice every day so that I can swim fast.

Please take a memo so that you will not forget.

examples.jp: 速く泳げるように、毎日練習しています。
忘れないように、メモしてください。
The
examples.vi: dictionary form of non-volitional verbs such as potential verbs, わかります, みえます,きこえます, なります, etc., or a verb in its negative form should be used before ように.

36.meaning: If you practice every day, you will become able to swim.
36.explanation:
If you practice every day, you will become able to swim.

I’ve finally become able to ride a bicycle.

As you get older, you cannot read small letters.

I gained weight so I can no longer wear my favorite dress.

Have you become able to play work by Chopin?

The Japanese started to eat beef and pork from about 100 years ago.

I do not walk much since I bought a car.

examples.jp: 1) なりますindicates
examples.vi: that a state changes into another state. The verbs used here express ability or possibility: such as potential verbs, わかります, みえます, etc. V dictionary form ようになりますindicates that a state in which something cannot be done has changed into a state in which it can be done. Vない-formなくなります indicates the reverse (i.e., something can no longer be done).

examples.jp: 毎日練習すれば、泳げるようになります。
やっと自転車に乗れるようになりました。
年を取ると、小さい字が読めなくなります。
太りましたから、好きな服が着られなくなりました。

examples.vi: How to give a negative answer to ～ようになりましたかis as follows:

examples.jp: ショパンが弾けるようになりましたか。
いいえ、まだ弾けません。
日本人は１００年ぐらいまえから牛肉や豚肉を食べるようになりました。
車を買ってから、わたしはあまり歩かなくなりました。
Such
examples.vi: verbs as なれる, ふとる, やせる, etc., which denotes change itself cannot be used in this sentence pattern.

36.meaning: This sentence pattern is used to express that one habitually or continuously makes efforts to do something or not to do something.
36.explanation:
This sentence pattern is used to express that one habitually or continuously makes efforts to do something or not to do something.

This expresses that one habitually and continuously tries to do something.

I try to take exercise every day and eat a variety of foods.

I try not to eat sweets because they are bad for my teeth.

When requesting someone to try to do something habitually and continuously.

Please try to eat more vegetables.

When politely requesting someone to try to do something in a one-off situation.

Please be sure not to be late tomorrow.

Excuse me, please pass me the salt.

examples.jp: 1) ～ようにしています
毎日運動して、何でも食べるようにしています。
歯に悪いですから、甘いものを食べないようにしています。
2) ～ようにしてください
～て／～ないでくださいare
examples.vi: direct request expressions, while ～ようにしてくださいis an indirect request expression and more polite than the former. This pattern is used as shown below.

examples.jp: もっと野菜を食べるようにしてください。
あしたは絶対に時間に遅れないようにしてください。
すみませんが、塩を取ってください。
ｘ すみませんが、塩を取るようにしてください。

36.meaning: What kind of sports do you do?
36.explanation:
What kind of sports do you do?

Well, playing tennis, swimming, and so on.

examples.jp: ～とかis
examples.vi: used to give some examples just like～や, but ～とかis more colloquial. Unlike や, とかsometimes comes after the last noun to be mentioned.

examples.jp: どんなスポーツをしていますか。
そうですね。テニスとか水泳とか…。

37.meaning: Something that moves (animals, cars, etc.) can replace person2 in this sentence pattern.
37.explanation:
Something that moves (animals, cars, etc.) can replace person2 in this sentence pattern.

When a person (person2)’s action is directed to an object belonging to another person (person 1), and person1 feels annoyed or troubled, person1 uses this sentence pattern to express his/her feelings.

Like in sentence pattern 2 above, an animate object or something that moves can replace person2.

When you need not mention the person who does the action denoted by a verb, you can make the object of the verb the subject of the sentence. In this case, a passive verb is used.

examples.vi: N1 (person1)は N2 (person2)に V passive

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: a person (person2)’s action is directed to another person (peson1), person1 can state it from his/her side by using this sentence pattern. In this case, person1 becomes the topic of the sentence, person2 is marked with に, and a passive verb is used.

examples.jp: 先生はわたしを褒めました。
examples.vi: My teacher praised me.

examples.jp: わたしは先生に褒められました。
examples.vi: I was praised by my teacher.

examples.jp: 母はわたしに買い物を頼みました。
examples.vi: My mother asked me to go shopping.

examples.jp: わたしは母に買い物を頼まれました。
examples.vi: I was asked to go shopping by my mother.

examples.jp: わたしは犬にかまれました。
examples.vi: I was bitten by a dog.
N1 (person1)は N2 (person2)にN3を V passive

examples.jp: 弟がわたしのパソコンを壊しました。
examples.vi: My brother broke my personal computer.

examples.jp: わたしは弟にパソコンを壊されました。(*)
examples.vi: I had my personal computer broken by my brother.

examples.jp: わたしは犬に手をかまれました。
examples.vi: I had my hand bitten by a dog.

examples.jp: わたしは友達に自転車を修理してもらいました。
examples.vi: I had my bicycle repaired by my friend.

examples.jp: ｘ わたしは友達に自転車を修理されました。

examples.vi: N (thing) が／はV passive

examples.jp: フランスで昔の日本の絵が発見されました。
examples.vi: An old Japanese picture has been discovered in France.

examples.jp: 日本の車は世界中へ輸出されています。
examples.vi: Japanese cars are exported all over the world.

examples.jp: 会議は神戸で開かれました。
examples.vi: The assembly was held in Kobe.
N1 はN2 (person) によってV passive

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: something is created or discovered, and it is stated using a passive verb, the person who created or discovered it is indicated by によってinstead ofに. Verbs like かきます, はつめいします, はっけんします, etc., are used in this sentence pattern.

examples.jp: 「源氏物語」は紫式部によって書かれました。 “The
examples.vi: Tale of Genji” was written by Murasaki Shikibu.

examples.jp: 電話はベルによって発明されました。
examples.vi: The telephone was invented by Bell.

37.meaning: Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 37 Vocabulary
37.explanation: Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 37 Vocabulary

examples.vi: Nから／ Nでつくります

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: something is made from a raw material, the material is marked withから. When it is obvious to the eye that something is made of a particular material, the material is marked with で.

examples.jp: ビールは麦から造られます。
examples.vi: Beer is made from barley.

examples.jp: 昔 日本の家は木で造られました。
examples.vi: Japanese houses were made of wood in the past.

38.explanation:
Do you know that …?

Do you know that Ms. Kimura had a baby?

Do you know Mr. Miller’s address?

Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 38 Vocabulary

examples.vi: V dictionary form のを忘れました

examples.jp: かぎを忘れました。
examples.vi: I forgot the key.

examples.jp: 牛乳を買うのを忘れました。
examples.vi: I forgot to buy milk.

examples.jp: 車の窓を閉めるのを忘れました。
examples.vi: I forgot to close the car window.
V plain formのを知っていますか。

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: is an expression asking whether the listener knows what is described in the clause preceding の.

examples.jp: 鈴木さんが来月結婚するのを知っていますか。
examples.vi: Do you know that Mr. Suzuki is going to get married next month?

examples.jp: 木村さんに赤ちゃんが生まれたのを知っていますか。(i)
いいえ、知りませんでした。
ミラーさんの住所を知っていますか。(ii)
いいえ、知りません。
しりませんでしたis
examples.vi: used in an example (i), because the person replying has got the information from the question. In example (ii), however, しりませんis used because the person replying has not got any information from the question.
Plain form のはNです

examples.jp: な-adj /
examples.vi: N：～だ → ～な

examples.jp: 娘は北海道の小さな町で生まれました。
examples.vi: My daughter was born in a small town in Hokkaido.

examples.jp: 娘が生まれたのは北海道の小さな町です。
examples.vi: My daughter’s birthplace is a small town in Hokkaido.

examples.jp: １２月は１年で一番忙しいです。
examples.vi: December is the busiest month of the year.

examples.jp: １年でいちばん忙しいのは１２月です。
examples.vi: The busiest month of the year is December.

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: pattern is used when a noun representing a thing, a person, a place, etc., is replaced with のand then taken up as the topic of the sentence.

examples.jp: 7. ～ときも／～ときや／～ときの／～ときに、etc.
Various
examples.vi: particles can be attached to ～とき, which you learned in Lesson 23, because the word ときis a noun.

examples.jp: 疲れたときや寂しいとき、田舎を思い出す。
examples.vi: I remember my hometown when I am tired or lonely.

examples.jp: 生まれたときから、ずっと大阪に住んでいます。
examples.vi: I have been living in Osaka since I was born.

39.meaning: 1) The words which come in the latter part are limited to those words which do not contain volition:
39.explanation:
1) The words which come in the latter part are limited to those words which do not contain volition:

– Potential verbs and verbs to express states:

– Situations in the past:

3) In this sentence pattern, the first part and the second part of the sentence are sequential events. In other words, the first part takes place first and the second part takes place after that.

As it is soft expression, it is not used with the imperative or the prohibitive forms.

Don’t touch the machine because it’s dangerous.

May I leave now? I have something to do.

Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 39 Vocabulary

examples.jp: In
examples.vi: this sentence pattern, the first part of the sentence presents a cause and the second part presents the consequence produced by the cause. Unlike ～からthat you learned in Lesson 9, this pattern has many constraints over its usage.
Verbs and adjectives to express feelings, such as びっくりする, あんしんする, こまる, さびしい, うれしい, ざんねんだ, etc.:

examples.jp: ニュースを聞いて、びっくりしました。
examples.vi: I was surprised to hear the news.

examples.jp: 家族に会えなくて、寂しいです。
examples.vi: I’m sad because I can’t meet my family

examples.jp: 土曜日は都合が悪くて、行けません。
examples.vi: Saturday is inconvenient for me, so I cannot come.

examples.jp: 話が複雑で、よくわかりませんでした。
examples.vi: The story was complicated, so I could not understand it well.

examples.jp: 事故があって、バスが遅れてしまいました。
examples.vi: The bus was delayed by an accident.

examples.jp: 授業に遅れて、先生にしかれました。
examples.vi: I was late for the lesson, so I was scolded by my teacher.

Expressions containing volition (will, orders, invitation or request) are not used in the latter part of the sentence. When the latter part of the sentence contains volition, the phrase with てcannot be used and instead the phrase withから is used.

examples.jp: 危ないですから、機械に触らないでください。
examples.vi: It is dangerous, so please do not touch the machine.

examples.jp: ｘ 危なくて、機械に触らないでください。
あした会議がありますから、きょう準備しなければなりません。
examples.vi: The meeting will be held tomorrow, so we have to make preparations for it today.

examples.jp: ｘ あした会議があって、きょう準備しなければなりません。

examples.vi: Nで

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: particleで that you learn in this lesson indicates a cause. Nouns used in this case are those which indicate natural phenomena, happenings, events such as じこ, じしん, かじ, etc. As with sentence pattern in 1., in this construction expressions containing volition are not used as predicates.

examples.jp: 地震でビルが倒れました。
examples.vi: Because of the earthquake, a building collapsed.

examples.jp: 病気で会社を休みました。
examples.vi: Because of illness, I took a day off work.

examples.jp: ｘ 病気であした会社を休みたいです。

examples.vi: Plain form ので、～

examples.jp: な-adj /
examples.vi: N：～だ à ～な

examples.jp: Like～から
examples.vi: that you learned in Lesson 9, ～のでindicates causes and reasons. While ～からsubjectively highlights a cause or a reason, ～のでobjectively presents a cause and effect relationship as a natural course of events. As the use of ～のでsoftens the view of the speaker, leaving a weak impact on the listener, it is often used to express a reason gently, to ask for permission or to make an excuse.

examples.jp: 日本語がわからないので、英語で話していただけませんか。
examples.vi: I don’t understand Japanese, so would you please speak in English?

examples.jp: 用事があるので、お先に失礼します。
examples.vi: May I leave now? I have something to do.

examples.jp: 危ないから、機械に触るな。
ｘ 危ないので、機械に触るな。
用事がありますので、お先に失礼します。
（＝用事があるので、お先に失礼します。）
4. 途中で
とちゅうでmeans “during”
examples.vi: or “on the way to”. It follows V dictionary form or N の.

examples.jp: 実は来る途中で事故があって、バスが遅れてしまったんです。
examples.vi: Actually, on my way here there was an accident and the bus was delayed.

examples.jp: マラソンの途中で気分が悪くなりました。
examples.vi: I got sick during the marathon.
Plain form + かどうか、～

examples.jp: な-adj /
examples.vi: N：～だ → ～な

examples.jp: A
examples.vi: question without an interrogative is used as a component of a sentence in this sentence pattern. Note that どうかis necessary after “plain form か“.

examples.jp: 忘年会に出席するかどうか、２０日までに返事をください。
examples.vi: Please answer by the 20th whether you’ll attend the year-end party or not.

examples.jp: その話はほんとうかどうか、わかりません。
examples.vi: I don’t know whether the story is true or not.

examples.jp: まちがいがないかどうか、調べてください。(*)
examples.vi: Please check if there are no mistakes.
not まちがいがあるかどうかbut まちがいがないかどうかis used before because the speaker wants to confirm that there are no mistakes.

40.meaning: This sentence pattern is used to show that the action denoted by the verb is a trial.
40.explanation: This sentence pattern is used to show that the action denoted by the verb is a trial.

examples.vi: Vて-form みます

examples.jp: もう一度考えてみます。
examples.vi: I’ll think it over again.

examples.jp: 宇宙から地球を見てみたいです。
examples.vi: I want to see the earth from space (to know how it looks).

examples.jp: このズボンをはいてみてもいいですか。
examples.vi: May I try on this pair of trousers?

examples.jp: 4. い-adj (～い) → ～さ
Change
examples.vi: the final いof an い-adjective into さ, and you can transform the adjective into a noun.

examples.jp: e.g., 高いà高さ 長い à長さ 速い à 速さ
山の高さはどうやって測るか、知っていますか。
examples.vi: Do you know how to measure the height of a mountain?

examples.jp: 新しい橋の長さは3,911メートです。
examples.vi: The new bridge is 3,911 meters long.

40.meaning: Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 40 Vocabulary
40.explanation: Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 40 Vocabulary

examples.jp: 5. ハンスは学校でどうでしょうか。
～でしょうか,
examples.vi: which is used to ask a question whose answer the listener might be uncertain of, is also used to ask a question whose answer the listener is sure to be able to give. In this case, it is euphemistic and hence more polite.

41.meaning: In Lesson 7 and 24, you learned expressions for the giving and receiving of things and actions. In this lesson, you will learn other expressions for giving and receiving things and actions, but these
41.explanation: In Lesson 7 and 24, you learned expressions for the giving and receiving of things and actions. In this lesson, you will learn other expressions for giving and receiving things and actions, but these reflect the relationship between the giver and the receiver.

examples.vi: N1に N2をやります

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: the receiver is a person of lower status or an animal or plant, やりますis normally used. However, when the receiver is a person, あげますis often preferred in current Japanese.

examples.jp: わたしは息子にお菓子をやりました（あげました）。
examples.vi: I gave some sweets to my son.

examples.jp: わたしはいぬにえさをやりました。
examples.vi: I gave some food to the dog.
N1に N2 をいただきます

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: the speaker receives a thing from a person of higher social status, いただきますis used instead of もらいます.

examples.jp: わたしは部長にお土産をいただきました。
examples.vi: I received a souvenir from the manager.

examples.jp: 3) [わたし]にNをくださいます
When
examples.vi: a person of higher social status gives the speaker something, くださいますis used instead of くれます.

examples.jp: 部長がわたしにお土産をくださいました。
examples.vi: The manager gave me a souvenir.

examples.jp: くださいますis
examples.vi: also used when the receiver is a member of the speaker’s family.

examples.jp: 部長が娘にお土産をくださいました。
examples.vi: The manager gave a souvenir to my daughter.

examples.jp: やります, いただきます,
examples.vi: and くださいますare also used in expressing the giving and receiving of actions. Examples are shown below.
Vて-formやります

examples.jp: わたしは息子に紙飛行機を作ってやりました（あげました）。
examples.vi: I made a paper plan for my son.

examples.jp: わたしは犬を散歩を連れて行ってやりました。
examples.vi: I took my dog for a walk.

examples.jp: わたしは娘の宿題を見てやりました（あげました）。
examples.vi: I checked my daughter’s homework.
Vて-form いただきます

examples.jp: わたしは課長に手紙のまちがいを直していただきました。
examples.vi: I had my letter corrected by section chief.
Vて-formくださいます

examples.jp: 部長の奥さんは[わたしに]お茶を教えてくださいました。
examples.vi: The manager’s wife taught me the tea ceremony.

examples.jp: 部長は[わたしを]駅まで送ってくださいました。
examples.vi: The manager took me to the station.

examples.jp: 部長は[わたしの]レポートを直してくださいました。
examples.vi: The manager corrected my report.

examples.jp: ～てくださいませんかis
examples.vi: a polite expression of request, although it is less polite than～ていただけませんか in Lesson 26.

examples.jp: コピー機の使い方を教えてくださいませんか。
examples.vi: Will you kindly show me how to use the photocopier?

examples.jp: コピー機の使い方を教えていただけませんか。
examples.vi: Would you kindly show me how to use the photocopier?

41.meaning: Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 41 Vocabulary
41.explanation: Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 41 Vocabulary

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: particleに means “as a token of” or “in memory of”.

examples.jp: 田中さんが結婚のお祝いにこのお皿をくださいました。
examples.vi: Mr. Tanaka gave this plate as my wedding gift.

examples.jp: わたしは北海道旅行のお土産に人形を買いました。
examples.vi: I bought a doll as a souvenir of the trip to Hokkaido.

42.pattern: in order to V for N
42.meaning: This sentence pattern indicates a purpose.
42.explanation:
This sentence pattern indicates a purpose.

I am saving money in order to be able to have my own shop.

(*) means that one has intentionally set up the objective of ‘having a shop” and “is saving” money to attain that objective, while (**) means one’s objective is a state in which “a shop may be gained” and one “is saving money” in order to get closer to that state.

examples.jp: 自分の店を持つために、貯金しています。
examples.vi: I am saving money in order to have my own shop.

examples.jp: 引っ越しのために、車を借ります。
examples.vi: I will rent a car for the move.

examples.jp: N のためにis
examples.vi: also used to mean “for the benefit of N”.

examples.jp: 健康のために、毎朝走っています。
examples.vi: For my health, I jog every morning.

examples.jp: 家族のために、うちを建てます。
examples.vi: I will build a house for my family.

examples.jp: 自分の店を持つために、貯金しています。(*)
自分の店を持てるように、貯金しています。(**)
弁護士になるために、法律を勉強しています。
examples.vi: I study law in order to become a lawyer.

examples.jp: 日本語が上手になるように、毎日勉強しています。
examples.vi: I study every day so that my Japanese may improve.

42.meaning: Let us summarize the expressions for indicating purpose that you have learned so far.
42.explanation: Let us summarize the expressions for indicating purpose that you have learned so far.

examples.jp: As
examples.vi: you learned in Lesson 38, V dictionary form のcan be used as a noun phrase. V dictionary form のにand N にare followed by such expressions as つかう, いい, べんりだ, やくにたつ, [じかんが]かかる, etc., and indicate a purpose.

examples.jp: このはさみは花を切るのに使います。
examples.vi: This pair of scissors is used to cut flowers.

examples.jp: このかばんは大きくて、旅行に便利です。
examples.vi: This bag is big and convenient for trips.

examples.jp: 電話番号を調べるのに時間がかかりました。
examples.vi: It took a lot of time to find the telephone number.

examples.jp: N に行きます／来ます／帰ります 神戸へ船を見に行きます。
examples.vi: I’ll go to Kobe to look at the ships.

examples.jp: 日本へ経済の勉強に来ました。
examples.vi: I came to Japan to study economics.

examples.jp: Vない-formない ように、 ～(volitional
examples.vi: expression) 早く届くように、速達で出しました。 I will mail this by special delivery so that it can get there earlier.

examples.jp: 忘れないように、メモします。
examples.vi: I make a note so that I don’t forget.

examples.jp: Nの ために、 ～(volitional
examples.vi: expression) 大学に入るために、一生懸命勉強します。 I study as hard as possible in order to enter university.

examples.jp: 健康のために、野菜をたくさん食べます。
examples.vi: I eat a lot of vegetables for my health.

examples.jp: N に使います／役に立ちます／[時間]がかかります
にいいです／便利です／必要です ファイルは書類を整理するのに使います。
examples.vi: Files are used to put paper in order.

examples.jp: 近くに店がなくて、買い物に不便です。
examples.vi: There are no stores nearby, so it is inconvenient for shopping.

examples.jp: When
examples.vi: attached to a quantifier, the particle はindicates the minimum amount that the speaker estimates is required or necessary.

examples.jp: 日本では結婚式をするのに２００万円は要ります。
examples.vi: In Japan you need at least 2 million yen to hold a wedding.

42.meaning: When attached to a quantifier, the particle indicates that the speaker thinks that the amount mentioned is a lot.
42.explanation:
When attached to a quantifier, the particle indicates that the speaker thinks that the amount mentioned is a lot.

Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 42 Vocabulary

examples.jp: 駅まで行くのに２時間もかかりました。
examples.vi: It took as long as two hours to get to the station.

examples.jp: うちを建てるのに３０００万円も必要なんですか。
examples.vi: You need as much as 30 million yen to build a house?

43.meaning: làm gì đó (rồi sẽ trở lại)
43.explanation:
Cách dùng: dùng để diễn đạt tình trạng, trái thái đang hiện hữu bề ngoài, nói lên 1 ấn tượng có được từ 1 hiện tượng mà mình nhìn thấy tận mắt hay giải thích về tình trạng sự vật, sự việc ngay trước khi nó thay đổi.

Cách dùng: biểu thị hành động đi đâu đó làm gì rồi sau đó quay lại. Điều cần chú ý ở mẫu câu này là với 1 câu nhưng bao hàm 3 hành động

Chú ý: Địa điểm đi đến và thực hiện hành động ở đó được thể hiện bằng trợ từ 「で」. Tuy nhiên, khi địa điểm đó là nơi lấy đi (hoặc di chuyển) một vật thì ta dùng trợ từ 「から」.

examples.jp: 曇っていますね。 雨が 降りそうです。
examples.vi: Bầu trời nhiều mây quá. Trời sắp mưa.

examples.jp: もうすぐ 桜が 咲きそうです。
examples.vi: Hoa sakura sắp nở.

examples.jp: これから 寒くなりそうです。
examples.vi: Trời sắp lạnh.

examples.jp: 今にも 荷物が 落ちそうです。
examples.vi: Hành lý sắp rơi.

examples.jp: 煙が 少なくなりました。 火事は 消えそうです。
examples.vi: Khói ít đi rồi. Cơn hỏa hoạn sắp tắt.

examples.jp: Ngữ pháp Ａ／Ｎａそうです
この 料理は おいしそうです。
examples.vi: Món ăn này có vẻ ngon.

examples.jp: 彼女は 忙しそうです。
examples.vi: Cô ấy có vẻ bận.

examples.jp: 彼は 暇そうです。
examples.vi: Anh ấy có vẻ rảnh rỗi.

examples.jp: あのケーキは おいしくなさそうです。
examples.vi: Cái bánh kia có vẻ không ngon.

examples.jp: ○ わたしは うれしいです。 × わたしは うれしそうです。
○ あなたは うれしいそうです × あなたは うれしいです。
○ かのじょは うれしそうです。 × かのじょは うれしいです。
Sự khác nhau giữa 「~でしょう」và「 ~そうです」:
Ví dụ : ちょっと たばこを 買って来ます。
examples.vi: Tôi đi mua thuốc lá một chút (rồi về).

examples.jp: ちょっと 電話を かけて来ますから、ここで 待っていて ください。
examples.vi: Vì tôi đi gọi điện một chút nên hãy chờ tôi ở đây

examples.jp: 教 室に 時計を 忘れたので、ちょっと 取って来ます。
examples.vi: Vì quên đồng hồ ở lớp học nên tôi sẽ đi lấy.

examples.jp: スーパーで 牛 乳を 買って来ます。
examples.vi: Tôi đi đến mua sữa ở siêu thị.

examples.jp: 台 所から コップを 取って来ます。
examples.vi: Tôi đi lấy cốc từ nhà bếp.

examples.jp: Sự khác nhau giữa 「(場所)へVに行く」 「(場所)でVて来る」
～すぎますindicates
examples.vi: that the degree of an action or a state is excessive. Therefore, it is usually used with reference to undesirable states.

examples.jp: ゆうべお酒を飲みすぎました。
examples.vi: I drank too much last night.

examples.jp: このセーターは大きすぎます。
examples.vi: This sweater is too large (for me).

examples.jp: ～すぎますis
examples.vi: classified as Group II verb and therefore conjugates in the same way.

examples.jp: e.g., のみすぎる、のみすぎ(ない)、のみすぎた
最近の車は操作が簡単すぎて、運転がおもしろくないです。
examples.vi: The handling of recent cars is so easy that driving them is not so enjoyable for me.

examples.jp: いくら好きでも、飲みすぎると、体に悪いですよ。
examples.vi: No matter how much you like it, drinking too much is bad for your health.

44.meaning: 1) These phrases depict the easiness or difficulty in handling or doing something.
44.explanation:
1) These phrases depict the easiness or difficulty in handling or doing something.

2) These phrases depict the easiness or difficulty in the characteristics of an object or a person changing or in the likelihood of something happening.

examples.jp: このパソコンは使いやすいです。
examples.vi: This personal computer is easy to use.

examples.jp: 東京は住みにくいです。
examples.vi: Living in Tokyo is difficult.

examples.jp: 白いシャツは汚れやすいです。
examples.vi: A white shirt gets dirty easily.

examples.jp: 雨の日は洗濯物が乾きにくいです。
examples.vi: Things won’t dry easily on a rainy day.

examples.jp: この薬は砂糖を入れると、飲みやすくなりますよ。
examples.vi: If you add some sugar to this medicine, it will be easier to take.

examples.jp: このコップは割れにくくて、安全ですよ。
examples.vi: This glass won’t break easily, so it is safe.

examples.jp: While ～く／～になりますthat
examples.vi: you learned in Lesson 19 indicates that something “turns into” a certain state,～く／ ～にします, as shown in the sentences below, indicates that somebody “turns” something into a certain state.

examples.jp: 音を大きくします。
examples.vi: I will turn up the volume.

examples.jp: 部屋をきれいにします。
examples.vi: I will clean my room.

examples.jp: 塩の量を半分にしました。
examples.vi: I reduced the amount of salt by half.

examples.jp: Nにしますexpresses
examples.vi: selection and/or decision.

examples.jp: 部屋はシングルにしますか、ツインにしますか。
examples.vi: Would you like a single room or a twin room?

examples.jp: 会議はあしたにします。
examples.vi: I will set the meeting for tomorrow.

44.meaning: When changing adjectives into the forms written above, they function as adverbs.
44.explanation:
When changing adjectives into the forms written above, they function as adverbs.

Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 44 Vocabulary

examples.jp: 野菜を細かく切ってください。
examples.vi: Please cut the vegetables into small pieces.

examples.jp: 電気や水は大切に使いましょう。
examples.vi: Let’s conserve electricity and water.

examples.jp: ～ばあいはis
examples.vi: an expression used to talk about a hypothetical situation. The sentence after it indicates how to cope with such a situation or its consequence. ばあいはfollows either verbs, adjectives or nouns. Because ばあいis a noun, the form of verbs, い-adjectives, な-adjectives and nouns connected to it are the same as the forms when modifying nouns.

examples.jp: 会議に間に合わない場合は、連絡してください。
examples.vi: If you cannot be in time for the meeting, please inform us.

examples.jp: 時間に遅れた場合は、会場に入れません。
examples.vi: If you are late, you will not be admitted to the hall.

examples.jp: ファクスの調子が悪い場合は、どうしたらいいですか。
examples.vi: In the event that the fax machine does not work well, what should I do?

examples.jp: 領収書が必要な場合は、係に言ってください。
examples.vi: When you need a receipt, please tell the person in charge.

examples.jp: 火事や地震の場合は、エレベーターを使わないでください。
examples.vi: In case of fire or earthquake, do not use the elevator.

45.meaning: In (*), the speaker expects that the woman will come because she promised to do so. So he naturally feels disappointed that she did not come. In (**), Sunday is normally a holiday, yet the speaker has
45.explanation:
In (*), the speaker expects that the woman will come because she promised to do so. So he naturally feels disappointed that she did not come. In (**), Sunday is normally a holiday, yet the speaker has to work so he feels dissatisfied. The second clause implies feelings of unexpectedness or dissatisfaction.

My room is small but clean.

Even if it rains tomorrow, I will go out.

You promised to come. Why didn’t you come?

Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 45 Vocabulary

examples.jp: のにfollows
examples.vi: either verbs, adjectives, or nouns. Their forms used with のにare plain form. のにis used when what is stated in the second clause runs contrary to what is expected from the first clause.

examples.jp: 約束をしたのに、彼女は来ませんでした。(*)
examples.vi: She did not come, even though she promised.

examples.jp: きょうは日曜日なのに、働かなければなりません。(**)
examples.vi: Even though today is Sunday, I have to work.

examples.jp: わたしの部屋は狭いですが、きれいです。(i)
（X狭いのに）
あした雨が降っても、出かけます。(ii)
（X雨が降るのに）
～がand ～てもin (i)
examples.vi: and (ii) cannot be substituted with ～のに. This is because (i) simply joins two different evaluations together and the second clause therein does not represent an unexpected consequence of what is stated in the first clause. The first clause of (ii) suggests a possibility, but ～のにcan only indicate things that have actually occurred in reality.

examples.jp: 約束をしたのに、どうして来なかったんですか。(iii)
（X約束をしましたが） （X約束をしても）
～のにin (iii)
examples.vi: cannot be substituted with ～がor ～ても. This is because the second clause expresses a strong reproach.

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: wordところ originally means “place”, but it is also used to indicate a temporal position. The ところthat you learn in this lesson is the latter and is used to stress a certain point in time during the course of action.
V dictionary formところです

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: sentence pattern shows that a person is about to start doing something or something is about to start. It may be used together with これから, [ちょうど]いまから, etc., which makes the meaning clearer.

examples.jp: 昼ごはんはもう食べましたか。
examples.vi: Have you had lunch yet?

examples.jp: いいえ、これから食べるところです。
examples.vi: No, I’m going to have it now.

examples.jp: 会議はもう始まりましたか。
examples.vi: Has the meeting begun yet?

examples.jp: いいえ、今から始まるところです。
examples.vi: No, it’s just beginning now.
Vて-formいるところです

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: sentence pattern shows that a person is now doing a certain action or a certain action is now being done. It is often used with いま.

examples.jp: 故障の原因がわかりましたか。
examples.vi: Do you know what caused the breakdown?

examples.jp: いいえ。今調べているところです。
examples.vi: No. We are investigating now.
Vた-form ところです

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: sentence pattern shows that a person has just finished a certain action or a certain action has just been completed. It is used together with たったいま, etc.

examples.jp: 渡辺さんはいますか。
examples.vi: Is Ms. Watanabe here?

examples.jp: あ、たった今帰ったところです。
examples.vi: Oh, she’s just left.

examples.jp: まだエレベーターの所にいるかもしれません。
examples.vi: She may be somewhere near the elevator.

examples.jp: たった今バスが出たところです。
examples.vi: The bus left just now.

examples.jp: もしもし田中ですが、今いいでしょうか。
examples.vi: Hello. This is Tanaka speaking. May I talk to you now?

examples.jp: すみません。今から出かけるところなんです。
examples.vi: Sorry. I’m just going out.

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: sentence pattern means that not much time has passed since a certain action or event occurred. It is the expression of the speaker’s feeling and can be used regardless of the real length of time that has passed if the speaker feels it is short. In this respect, this sentence pattern is different from Vた-formところです, which can only indicate the time when a certain action has just been completed.

examples.jp: さっき昼ごはんを食べたばかりです。
examples.vi: I had lunch only a while ago.

examples.jp: 木村さんは先月この会社に入ったばかりです。
examples.vi: Ms. Kimura joined this company only a month ago.

examples.jp: このビデオは先週買ったばかりなのに、調子がおかしいです。
examples.vi: I bought this video only a week ago, but it isn’t working well.

46.meaning: Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 46 Vocabulary
46.explanation: Related Post: Minna no Nihongo Lesson 46 Vocabulary

examples.jp: The
examples.vi: speaker uses this sentence pattern to show he/she is convinced of what is stated before はずです. By using this sentence pattern, the speaker implies that he/she has grounds to think so, that it is his/her own judgement, and that he/she is quite sure of it.

examples.jp: ミラーさんはきょう来るでしょうか。(*)
examples.vi: Do you think Mr. Miller will come today?

examples.jp: 来るはずですよ。きのう電話がありましたから。
examples.vi: I’m sure he’ll come. I received a phone call from him yesterday.

the grounds for the speaker’s judgement is yesterday’s phone call. Based on this call, the speaker himself judges that Mr. Miller will come today. The speaker shows his/her firm belief in this judgement by using ～はずです.

47.meaning: In example (**) the information source is Mr. Miller himself, while in example (*) it is highly possible that the information source is not necessarily Mr. Miller but somebody else. Another difference
47.explanation: In example (**) the information source is Mr. Miller himself, while in example (*) it is highly possible that the information source is not necessarily Mr. Miller but somebody else. Another difference is that in example (**) the words which Mr. Miller said can be quoted directly or indirectly. In example (*) on the other hand, only the plain form may be used.

examples.jp: This
examples.vi: is an expression for conveying information you have obtained from another source without adding your own point of view. When the source of information is given, it is indicated by～によると (according to), placed at the beginning of the sentence.

examples.jp: 天気予報によると、あしたは寒くなるそうです。
examples.vi: According to the weather forecast, it will be cold tomorrow.

examples.jp: クララさんは子どものとき、フランスに住んでいたそうです。
examples.vi: I heard that Klara lived in France when she was a child.

examples.jp: バリ島はとてもきれいだそうです。
examples.vi: I hear that Bali is very beautiful.

examples.jp: 雨が降りそうです。
examples.vi: It looks like rain.

examples.jp: 雨が降るそうです。
examples.vi: I heard that it will rain.

examples.jp: この料理はおいしそうです。
examples.vi: This food looks delicious.

examples.jp: この料理はおいしいそうです。
examples.vi: I heard that this food is delicious.

examples.jp: ミラーさんはあした京都へ行くそうです。(*)
examples.vi: I hear that Mr. Miller is going to Kyoto tomorrow.

examples.jp: ミラーさんはあした京都へ行くと言っていました。(**)
examples.vi: Mr. Miller said that he is going to Kyoto tomorrow.

47.meaning: Examples (i) indicates an intuitive judgement based on what the speaker has seen of Mr. Miller’s condition or behavior, and example (ii) indicates the speaker’s judgement based on what he has read, he
47.explanation: Examples (i) indicates an intuitive judgement based on what the speaker has seen of Mr. Miller’s condition or behavior, and example (ii) indicates the speaker’s judgement based on what he has read, heard or been told.

examples.jp: ～ようですconveys
examples.vi: the speaker’s subjective conjecture, which is based on the information obtained through his/her sensory organs.

examples.jp: A
examples.vi: sentence that ends in ようですsometimes accompaniesどうも which suggests the speaker cannot be certain if what he/she is saying is a fact.

examples.jp: 人が大勢集まっていますね。
examples.vi: Look, there is a big crowd.

examples.jp: 事故のようです。パトカーと救急車が来ていますよ。
examples.vi: It looks like there’s been an accident. A patrol car and an ambulance are there.

examples.jp: せきも出るし、頭も痛い。どうもかぜをひいたようだ。
examples.vi: I have a cough and a headache. It looks like I’ve caught a cold.

examples.jp: ミラーさんは忙しそうです。(i)
examples.vi: Mr. Miller seems to be busy.

examples.jp: ミラーさんは忙しいようです。(ii)
examples.vi: It seems that Mr. Miller is busy.

examples.jp: 変な音がしますね。

examples.jp: A
examples.vi: phenomenon that is perceivable by the senses is described by using ～がします. Expressions in this category are こえがします, においがします, and あじがします. All these expressions mean that these things have been perceived or sensed regardless of the speaker’s intention.

examples.jp: します こさせます
させます こさせる
させる
All
